summary: Round 3: specialized/deep-method battery of the Kamikakushi PRD (T0 sim, system-interaction matrix, type-level data-model, number-spine re-derivation, naming concordance, impl-readiness, inspiration-game gaps, prose/tone, T3/T4 coherence, generate-dont-duplicate, determinism replay, completeness critic); dedup vs rounds 1-2
logs: Round 3: launching 12 deep-method auditors (dedup vs 97 known findings)...

confirmedFindings.title: T0 Estate gate's 70% proof spends 72 ip on TRADE contracts that cannot exist in T0 (trade strand opens at T1-V3; 'no market' in T0)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.2.1's T0 Estate 70%=560 itemization (line 2410): '...+ 6 trade contracts (6×12 = 72) = 30 + 224 + 234 + 72 = 560 ✔. (Trade = 72 ip = 12.9 %...)', and the deed table (line 2398) gives the 'Sealed trade contract (TRADE strand — ≤⅓ capped)' a T0 base of 12. But trade/market does not exist in T0: §3.2 line 1807 lists 'coin/*mon* (a T1 reveal — there is no market yet)' as deliberately withheld, §4.8.2 V3 line 2990 says the 'trade strand opens (≤⅓-capped)' only at the T1 rung V3, §4.7.1 marks the trade good 'Sericulture (silk, T1+)', and §1.7 line 446 places the Market/Shop Row at T1. With no T0 market the 6 trade contracts (72 ip) cannot be sealed, so T0 Estate deed-ip = 560−72 = 488; combined with the 240 seasonal it reaches only 728, short of the §4.1 Estate ≥0.8K T0→T1 gate. The headline gate proof that rounds 1-2 treated as the SOUND (Estate) side is therefore invalid as itemized.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The T0→T1 Estate gate (0.8K, a hard tier gate) is, as written, unreachable from the deed inventory the PRD uses to prove it, because 12.9% of that inventory is a deed class the game does not unlock until the next tier. This also contradicts a LOCKED reveal-ordering ('no market in T0').
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide whether T0 should expose a limited rice-sale/barter trade (raising it above the 'no market in T0' lock) or re-itemize the T0 Estate 560-ip inventory without any trade contracts (e.g. add ~3 solvency or ~5 yield deeds to cover the 72 ip), and update the §4.2.1 worked sum and the 12.9% trade-share line accordingly.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed in source. The §4.2.1 deed table (line 2398) assigns the 'Sealed trade contract (TRADE strand — ≤⅓ capped)' a T0 base of 12, and the T0 Estate 70%=560 itemization (line 2410) spends 6×12=72 ip on trade contracts. This 560-ip figure is the deed half of the locked T0→T1 Estate ≥0.8K gate proof (§4.8.1 lines 2967-2968: '70% = 560 ip from 30 deeds + 30% = 240 ip'). But the SAME 'TRADE strand ≤⅓-capped' mechanic is explicitly stated to OPEN only at T1 rung V3 (§4.8.2 line 2990), the Market/Shop Row and silk (Weaver Onatsu) are T1 (§1.7 line 446), the silk meibutsu 'lives entirely inside this capped strand' (§4.2.3 line 2591), and §3.2 line 1807 LOCKS 'no market yet' in T0 ('coin/mon — a T1 reveal'). So 72 of the 560 deed-ip come from a deed class that, by the PRD's own reveal ordering, cannot exist in T0. Removing them yields 488 deed-ip; with 240 seasonal the gate reaches only 728 < 800, so the centrepiece gate proof is internally unreachable as itemized. This is a genuine internal inconsistency (not a math error — 560 sums correctly — but a conceptual/reveal-ordering contradiction) and is distinct from generic gate-proof findings because it isolates the trade-strand-in-T0 conflict. Needs-human because the fix is a design choice (open a limited T0 barter strand vs re-itemize the 560 without trade), not a clerical swap.
confirmedFindings.question: The T0 Estate gate proof (§4.2.1 line 2410 / §4.8.1) spends 72 of its 560 deed-ip (12.9%) on 6 'sealed trade contracts' at T0 base 12 (§4.2.1 line 2398), but the TRADE strand is a LOCKED T1 reveal: §3.2 says 'no market yet' in T0, §1.7 puts the Market/Shop Row at T1, and §4.8.2 V3 says 'the trade strand opens (≤⅓-capped)' only at T1. Removing trade drops T0 Estate deeds to 488 ip, so the gate (deeds 560 + seasonal 240 = 800) falls to 728 and is unreachable as itemized. Which do you want: (A) open a limited T0 rice-sale/barter trade strand (raises it above the locked 'no market in T0' constraint — note this also needs the deed table's T0 trade base and the V3 'opens' language reconciled), or (B) keep trade T1-only and re-itemize the T0 Estate 560-ip inventory using only LAND/TREASURY deeds (e.g. +3 solvency steps at 26 = 78, or +5 yield milestones at 16 = 80 ≈ the 72 gap), updating the §4.2.1 worked sum, the 12.9% trade-share line, and the §4.8.1 references accordingly?

confirmedFindings.title: Koku held-vs-produced is self-contradictory: throughput is defined NET of food/re-investment, which makes held ≈19K, yet the PRD asserts held ≈3-5K and proves affordability off lifetime-produced (double-count)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.7.1 (line 2855-2858) defines the rung rates as 'the *net koku-equivalent throughput* (after stamina, food, and re-investment)' and says 'lifetime-produced koku over T0 ≈ 21K (held ≈ 3–5K after spend)'. If the 21.5K is already NET of food/re-investment, the only remaining sink is E1+E2 = 400+2,000 = 2,400 koku, so held = 21,500 − 2,400 ≈ 19,100 — not 3–5K. To land at 3–5K held you must subtract another ~16K of food/tools, i.e. treat 21.5K as GROSS — contradicting the 'net … after … food, and re-investment' label. Compounding it, §4.7.5 (lines 2906-2909) proves affordability against LIFETIME-produced ('E2's 2K koku falls due around R6, by which point lifetime-produced koku ≈ 13.3K … comfortably affordable'), but the player spends from HELD, not lifetime. On any held trajectory ending at the stated 3–5K, held at R6 (after 13.25K lifetime, held/produced ≈0.14–0.23) is only ~1.8–2.7K against E2's 2K cost — i.e. NOT 'comfortably affordable', with soft-lock risk at the low end since building E2 is R6's stated gating cost to advance.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The single thing affordability and soft-lock depend on — held koku per rung — is never modelled, and the two figures that bound it (net throughput → 19K held; stated 3–5K held) are mutually exclusive. The affordability claim that round-1's impossible-gate work relied on is proved with the wrong quantity (lifetime not held), so a R6 soft-lock on E2 cannot be ruled out.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one accounting basis. Either (a) declare throughput GROSS and add an explicit per-rung food/tools/crafting price line so a held-koku ledger can be computed and E2@R6 affordability verified from held, or (b) keep throughput NET and correct the held figure to ~19K (lifetime − buildings). Then re-state §4.7.5 affordability against held koku, not lifetime-produced.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed genuine internal contradiction. The same 21.5K figure is labeled both "net koku-equivalent throughput (after stamina, food, and re-investment)" (§4.7.1 L2855; §4.8.1 L2949) and "lifetime koku produced" (§4.8.1 L2964; §4.0 L2284), while held is asserted at ~3–5K. The throughput×wall-clock math ties out exactly to 21.5K, so these labels are mutually exclusive: if the rate is NET of food/re-investment, held = 21.5K − E1/E2(2.4K) ≈ 19.1K; to reach 3–5K held the 21.5K must be GROSS (subtract ~14–16K food/tools), contradicting the "net after food" label. The 2026-06-25 §4.0 fix only reconciled 21K vs 21.5K rounding, not this net/gross-held question, so it is unaddressed and NEW. §4.7.5 also proves E1/E2 affordability against lifetime-produced (5.1K@R4, 13.3K@R6) though the player spends from held, and the held-per-rung trajectory is modeled nowhere — so E2@R6 affordability from held is genuinely unverified and a R6 soft-lock on a gating build cannot be ruled out. Rated medium rather than high: the contradiction is certain but sits in an explicitly "proposed" balance section that already flags held as "tight by design," and the soft-lock is plausible-but-unproven. Needs-human because picking the canonical accounting basis and adding a held-koku ledger is a balance-design judgement, not a clerical fix.
confirmedFindings.question: Is the §4.7.1/§4.8.1 per-rung koku throughput GROSS production or NET of food/tools/re-investment? The doc uses both labels for the same 21.5K integral, and the two readings imply incompatible end-T0 held balances (~19K if net, ~3–5K if gross). Decide one accounting basis and reconcile: OPTION A — declare throughput GROSS, strip the "net (after stamina, food, and re-investment)" parenthetical at L2855/L2949, and add an explicit per-rung food/tools/crafting price line so a held-koku ledger can be computed and E2@R6 affordability verified from HELD (not lifetime); OPTION B — keep throughput NET and correct the §4.0/§4.8.1 held figure to ~19K (lifetime − buildings), dropping the 3–5K band. Either way, re-state the §4.7.5 affordability tie-out against held koku, and specify the held-per-rung trajectory used to confirm E2's 2K cost is affordable at R6 (where it gates advancement), so soft-lock is ruled out. Trade-off: Option A preserves the "held is tight, by design" feel but requires authoring a spend model; Option B is a one-line numeric correction but discards the tight-held design intent.

confirmedFindings.title: §4.8.0 'first meaningful reveal < 30 s' example names the Skills tab, but the Skills tab is an R2 reveal (~35-65 min); the sub-30s reveal is the koku panel
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.8.0 (line 2930): 'First meaningful reveal | < 30 s (rice counter ticks → Skills tab fades in) | proposed | first `unlock` event'. But the Skills tab is unambiguously an R2 reveal everywhere else: §3.2 R2 (line 1800) 'The Skills tab (§2.7) — first nav tab'; §3.5 (line 1960) 'First tab: "Skills" | RANK R2'; §7.2 M1 DoD (lines 4492, 4506) 'a headless run via __qa reaches R2, the Skills tab fades in on schedule'. R2 begins after R0(~5min)+R1(~30min), i.e. ~35-65 min in — not <30 s. The actual <30 s reveal is the koku/rice readout from the cold open §3.1 (line 1776: 'the koku row lights its own panel'), which is the genuine 'first unlock event'.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is a QA acceptance criterion measured by the harness (first `unlock` event). As written, a headless pacing check keyed to 'Skills tab fades in < 30 s' would FALSE-FAIL, because the Skills tab legitimately appears ~tens of minutes in at R2.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Correct the §4.8.0 example reveal from the Skills tab to the koku/rice panel that the cold open (§3.1 step 4) actually lights within seconds.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md line 2930: the §4.8.0 'First meaningful reveal | <30 s' example reads '(rice counter ticks -> Skills tab fades in)'. But the Skills tab is unambiguously an R2 reveal everywhere else (§3.2 R2 line 1800 'The Skills tab (§2.7) — first nav tab'; §3.5 line 1960 'First tab: "Skills" | RANK R2'), which occurs after R0+R1 (tens of minutes in), not <30 s. The actual sub-30s reveal is the koku panel at cold-open step 4 (§3.1 line 1776: 'The koku row lights its own panel'). The example thus contradicts the rest of the PRD. Severity reduced to low because the binding measurement column ('first unlock event') is correct and would fire on the koku panel, so the harness would NOT actually false-fail — only the illustrative prose is wrong. Clerical single-line fix; no design judgement.
confirmedFindings.old_string: **< 30 s** (rice counter ticks → Skills tab fades in)
confirmedFindings.new_string: **< 30 s** (rice counter ticks → the koku row lights its own panel, §3.1)

confirmedFindings.title: Dents × seasonal high-water gating: the stated dent self-heal is mechanically impossible
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.2.4 promises a dent 'never touches `highWater` (so it self-heals as play resumes the appraisal)' (line 2599) and that '`value` regrows toward `highWater` at the normal accrual rate — clawed back within ~1–2 seasons by design' (lines 2602-2603). But §4.2.2 gates the seasonal stream strictly: it 'raises the pillar only if the score exceeds its stored `highWater`' and 'If the score does not beat the high-water mark, nothing accrues (no maintenance trickle)' (lines 2443-2444); the code reads `if seasonalScore > influence[pillar].highWater` (line 2456). After a 0.10 dent, value = 0.9·highWater while highWater AND the underlying basis are unchanged — so next season seasonalScore == highWater (not strictly greater) → Δ=0, nothing accrues. The very fact that the dent leaves highWater untouched is exactly why the appraisal can NEVER fire to heal it. The only un-gated regrowth path is fresh achievement JUMPS (§4.2.1, value += jump, not HW-gated) — i.e. active re-grinding new deeds, not 'play resumes the appraisal' and not 'within 1–2 seasons.' Compounds at tier-end where the deed inventory is largely spent (the ~30 deeds were consumed to reach the gate), and for a defence-loss Arms dent (§4.6.6 line 2819) whose only refill deeds are more DEFEND/clears.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The 'recoverable dent, never a wipe, self-heals' promise is canon (§1.6.2, ADR D-015). If the only heal path is manual re-grinding of fresh deeds, dents are NOT self-recovering and a player who exhausted a tier's deed inventory before a dent is stuck below their earned standing indefinitely — silently violating the up-only/never-a-wipe intent.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide the heal mechanism and state it: either (a) add an explicit below-high-water restore branch to the seasonal appraisal (value regrows toward highWater each season WITHOUT advancing highWater — note this is a bounded maintenance award, which the canon currently forbids), or (b) drop the 'self-heals via the appraisal / within 1–2 seasons' language and state plainly that a dent is recovered only by fresh recognized deeds (active re-grind), with a guaranteed always-available deed so it cannot dead-end.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed real and new. §4.2.2's seasonal appraisal (lines 2456-2459) raises `value` ONLY on a strictly-new high-water mark (`if seasonalScore > highWater`); grep confirms `value +=` exists nowhere else and there is no below-highWater restore branch. A 0.10 dent (§4.2.4) leaves `highWater` and the judged basis untouched, so the next appraisal yields seasonalScore == highWater (not greater) → Δ not applied → the dent is never refilled. Even after the basis later grows, new Δ stacks on top of the dented value, so the gap persists permanently; `value` never climbs back to a static `highWater` via the appraisal. Therefore §4.2.4's prose ('self-heals as play resumes the appraisal', 'regrows toward `highWater` at the normal accrual rate — clawed back within ~1–2 seasons', line 2599/2602-2603) is mechanically contradicted by the gated code — the only un-gated heal path is fresh deed jumps (§4.2.1), i.e. active re-grind, not the appraisal. Distinct from the F1/F2/F4 fixes; not reconciled elsewhere. Severity reduced to medium because dents are rare, scripted, minor (10%), and value still grows by other channels, so the player is not catastrophically stuck — but it does violate the canon self-heal promise and needs a design decision (maintenance-restore branch vs. prose rewrite), hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: How does a dented pillar's `value` actually return to its untouched `highWater`? The seasonal appraisal (§4.2.2) increments `value` only when `seasonalScore > highWater`; after a dent the basis and `highWater` are unchanged, so `seasonalScore == highWater` and the appraisal never refills the dent — meaning the §4.2.4 'self-heals via the appraisal / clawed back within 1–2 seasons' promise cannot fire. Choose the heal mechanism and make §4.2.2/§4.2.4 agree: (a) add an explicit below-high-water restore branch (e.g. `if value < highWater: value = min(highWater, value + restoreRate)` each season, NOT advancing `highWater`) — note this is a bounded maintenance award, which the seasonal canon ('no maintenance trickle', line 2444) currently forbids, so the no-maintenance rule would need a scoped exception for dent recovery; or (b) drop the 'self-heals as play resumes the appraisal / within 1–2 seasons' language and state plainly that a dent is recovered only by fresh recognized deed-jumps (active re-grind, §4.2.1), and guarantee an always-available repeatable deed per dentable pillar so recovery can never dead-end when a tier's authored deed inventory is spent.

confirmedFindings.title: Soft-stamina × combat: §2.3 says low satiety slows combat, but the §4.6 combat math has no satiety term
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.3(b): 'As the MC labours or fights, satiety/energy drains; depleted, actions get slower / less efficient' (line 931), and §2.3(c): 'a soft-throttle function maps low satiety → a rate multiplier on labour/combat speed (never to zero)' (line 941). §6.3 confirms satiety drains during the per-tick combat sub-step (line 4014). But the §4.6.1 combat formulas — `attackSpeed = baseSpeed · (1 + 0.005·SPD) · stance.speedMod` and every other derived stat (lines 2758-2766) — contain NO satiety/soft-stamina factor, whereas the labour formula §4.7.1 explicitly multiplies by '(soft-stamina rate)' (line 2837). So the throttle is wired into labour but not combat: combat either is silently un-throttled (contradicting §2.3) or applies an undefined satiety multiplier.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is a load-bearing pairwise composition: if low satiety multiplies the player's attackSpeed but not the enemy's, a starving MC loses fights he'd otherwise win (a real difficulty/soft-setback consequence). Whether combat is throttled changes win-rates, the locked 20–35%→85% first-fight curve, and whether the 'eat before you fight' loop exists at all — none of which is currently specified.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add an explicit satiety term to §4.6.1 (e.g. multiply attackSpeed and/or accuracy by the same soft-stamina rate used in §4.7.1) and state it cannot drop the player below a floor, OR amend §2.3 to say the throttle applies to labour only and combat is satiety-independent.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed inconsistency. §2.3(c) (lines 940-941) explicitly states the soft-throttle maps low satiety to a rate multiplier on "labour/combat speed (never to zero)", and §6.3 (line 4014) confirms satiety drains during the per-tick combat sub-step. Yet the §4.6.1 combat formulas (lines 2758-2766: attackSpeed/accuracy/etc.) contain no satiety/soft-stamina term, whereas the §4.7.1 labour formula (line 2837) explicitly multiplies yieldPerAction by "(soft-stamina rate)". The M1 DoD (line 4502) further scopes the throttle to labour only. So the throttle is wired into labour but absent from combat, contradicting §2.3/§6.3. §2.8(c) line 1112 ("HP/satiety managed via rest/eat between fights") hints at intent but does not resolve the formula-level gap. This is load-bearing because whether a starving MC's attackSpeed/accuracy is throttled (and whether the enemy's is too, and what floor applies) directly affects the LOCKED first-fight 20-35%→85% win-rate and the existence of an "eat before you fight" loop. Not a duplicate of the soft-stamina-rate magnitude deferral (line 2188), which defers only the numeric value, not the structural question of which formulas the rate enters. Requires a design decision (which combat stat(s), floor, enemy symmetry), so needs-human rather than clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: Does low satiety throttle combat as §2.3(c)/§6.3 imply, or should the throttle be scoped to labour only? Options: (A) Add an explicit soft-stamina term to §4.6.1 — decide which stat(s) it multiplies (attackSpeed only, or also accuracy), whether it applies symmetrically to enemies, and the floor (e.g. never below 0.X× so combat is never near-zero), accepting this strengthens the "eat before you fight" loop and may shift the LOCKED 20-35% first-fight win-rate (so the wolf tuning must assume a stated satiety state). (B) Amend §2.3(c) and §6.3 to state the throttle applies to labour speed only and combat is satiety-independent (satiety merely drains in combat and is managed via rest/eat between fights per §2.8c), keeping the locked combat curve untouched. Trade-off: (A) adds a survival-resource layer to combat but complicates the locked win-rate; (B) is simpler and preserves locked balance but removes any in-fight stakes from satiety.

confirmedFindings.title: GameState.influence shape cannot store the estateWealth sub-engines (land/treasury/trade) that §2.16 declares and the trade ≤⅓ verifier reads
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.4 line 4053 vs §2.16(c) line 1472 and §6.6 line 4122
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.4 types influence as `influence: Record<PillarId, { value: number; highWater: number; dent: Dent | null }>` — each pillar is one scalar `value`. But §2.16(c) declares `Influence { arms, estateWealth (subEngines: { land, treasury, trade(≤⅓ cap enforced) }), office, name }`, and §6.6's verifier asserts "the **trade** sub-engine of Estate & Wealth is capped at **≤⅓** of that pillar." The trade strand accrues via up-only recognized deeds/judged results (historical, high-watermarked) and is therefore NOT derivable from current resource counts — it must be stored. There is no field anywhere in GameState in which the trade (or land/treasury) sub-value lives, so the ≤⅓ invariant has nothing to read.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The single hardest structural canon invariant (trade ≤⅓) is machine-checked by the verifier and unit-tested in M2b/M3, but the persisted data model has no field to evaluate it against. Either the invariant cannot be implemented or estateWealth.value silently conflates four sub-values, defeating the cap.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide the stored shape for estateWealth: nest the sub-engines under the estateWealth pillar entry, e.g. influence[estateWealth] = { value, highWater, dent, subEngines: { land, treasury, trade } }, or store a parallel `subEngines: Record<SubEngineId, number>`. Update §6.4 and the §6.8 persisted-surface list to match §2.16(c).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed real and new. §6.4 (line 4053) types influence as `Record<PillarId, { value; highWater; dent }>` — one scalar per pillar — and §6.8 (line ~4166) and §2.19(c)/(d) (lines 1607/1613) independently describe the persisted Influence the same way (pillar value + high-water + dents). None hold a land/treasury/trade breakdown. Yet §2.16(c) line 1472 declares `estateWealth (subEngines: { land, treasury, trade })`, and §4.2.3 lines 2572-2575 require `tradeAllowed = 0.5·(land+treasury)` applied 'at every accrual point' — operands that cannot be reconstructed from a single rolled-up estateWealth.value. The trade strand accrues via up-only, high-watermarked recognized deeds (line 2398), so it is not derivable from current resource counts and must be stored. M2b (line 4589) and M5 (line 4623) unit-test this clamp at every accrual point, so the gap is load-bearing, not cosmetic. The contradiction spans §2.16(c)/§4.2.3 vs §6.4/§6.8/§2.19 and is not reconciled anywhere. Note: the finding's claim that the §6.6 content verifier 'reads' the sub-value is imprecise (verify-content.ts checks static registries, not runtime GameState), but the runtime clamp and milestone tests independently establish the gap. Severity downgraded from high to medium: this is a spec-internal data-model inconsistency that any implementer resolves by storing the sub-engines, not a runtime defect that would silently ship a broken invariant. needs-human because the stored shape is a genuine design choice (nested subEngines on the pillar entry vs a parallel map; whether each sub-engine carries its own high-water/dent).
confirmedFindings.question: The trade≤⅓ clamp (§4.2.3) needs land/treasury/trade tracked separately and persisted (the trade strand is up-only, high-watermarked from discrete deeds, so it is not derivable from resource counts), but the canonical persisted shape in §6.4/§6.8/§2.19 stores only a single scalar `value` per pillar — with no field for the Estate & Wealth sub-engines that §2.16(c) declares. How should the sub-engine breakdown be stored so the ≤⅓ invariant is evaluable? Options: (a) nest under the estateWealth pillar entry, e.g. `influence[estateWealth] = { value, highWater, dent, subEngines: { land, treasury, trade } }` — keeps it local but makes the pillar entry non-uniform across the four pillars; (b) a parallel `subEngines: Record<SubEngineId, number>` map alongside influence — uniform pillar entries but a second source of truth to keep in sync with estateWealth.value; (c) make estateWealth.value a DERIVED roll-up of stored sub-engines (store sub-engines + per-strand high-water, derive the pillar total) — cleanest for the clamp but changes §6.4's 'store the pillar value' contract. Also decide whether each sub-engine needs its own high-water/dent. Whichever is chosen, §6.4's type, the §6.8 persisted-surface list, and §2.19(c)/(d) must all be updated to match §2.16(c).

confirmedFindings.title: Type-identity drift: equip is dispatched with ItemId but stored as EquipDefId, with no declared mapping between the two id spaces
confirmedFindings.evidence: The equip intent is `{ type: 'equip'; itemId: ItemId; slot: EquipSlot }` (line 3990) and inventory is `Record<ItemId, number>` (line 4051). But the stored equipment slot is `{ equipDefId: EquipDefId; durability; qualityTier }` (line 4052) and §2.10(c) `EquipState { slot → { equipDefId, … } }`. Meanwhile §6.5 lists ONE registry `content/items.ts … keyed by ItemId` that holds 'items/equipment/consumables' — there is no separate EquipDef registry, yet §2.10(c) names the type `EquipDef { id, slot, … }`. So a piece of gear is identified as `ItemId` (intent + inventory + registry key) in three places and as `EquipDefId` in two, with no statement that EquipDefId ⊆ ItemId or how reduce() maps one to the other.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: reduce() must translate the equip intent's ItemId into the stored equipDefId; if the two id spaces are distinct, the mapping is undefined; if identical, the second type name is dead drift that the strict compiler will treat as an unrelated branded type.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one identifier for equippable gear. Simplest: drop EquipDefId and type the slot as `{ itemId: ItemId; durability; qualityTier }`, or formally declare `type EquipDefId = ItemId` (narrowed to slot-bearing items) and say so at §6.4.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed verbatim: the equip intent carries `itemId: ItemId` (line 3990), inventory is `Record<ItemId, number>` (4051), and the single content registry `content/items.ts` holding "items/equipment/consumables" is keyed by `ItemId` (4094) — yet the stored equipment slot is `{ equipDefId: EquipDefId; … }` (4052) and §2.10(c) uses `EquipState { slot → { equipDefId, … } }` (1194) plus a type `EquipDef { id, slot, … }` (1192). grep confirms `EquipDefId` is referenced only in those two type-shape lines and is NEVER defined; nothing states `EquipDefId = ItemId` or how `reduce()` maps the dispatched ItemId to the stored equipDefId. Genuine type-identity drift in the canonical data model, not a restatement of other findings. Set to low (not the claimed medium) because the intended answer is strongly implied — equipment lives in the ItemId-keyed items.ts, so EquipDefId is almost certainly ItemId — but it is not declared, and choosing the canonical id space is a design decision, hence needs-human rather than trivial-apply.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Is an equippable item identified by `ItemId` (shared with inventory, the equip intent, and the items.ts registry) or by a distinct `EquipDefId`? Options: (A) Drop EquipDefId — declare equipment is just slot-bearing items, retype the stored slot as `{ itemId: ItemId; durability; qualityTier }` and rename §2.10(c) `equipDefId`→`itemId` (simplest; matches items.ts being keyed by ItemId and the equip intent carrying itemId; no mapping needed). (B) Keep a distinct EquipDefId — then §6.4/§6.5 must add `type EquipDefId = ItemId` (a narrowed brand for slot-bearing items) plus a one-line statement of how `reduce()` derives the stored equipDefId from the intent's itemId. Recommend (A) unless a separate equipment registry distinct from items.ts is actually intended.
confirmedFindings.question: Is an equippable item identified by ItemId (shared with inventory) or by a distinct EquipDefId? If distinct, what maps the equip intent's itemId to the stored equipDefId?

confirmedFindings.title: Live-combat state type CombatEncounterState has no field sketch, and combat_action's payload type (CombatAction) drifts from §2.8's CombatInterventionIntent
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.4 line 4063, §7 M2a line 4532, §6.3 line 3993, §2.8(c) lines 1122-1124
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.4 stores `combat?: CombatEncounterState` and M2a (line 4532) lists CombatEncounterState as a type core/combat 'exports', but §2.8(c) only sketches `Combatant`, `Stance { attackMod, defMod, speedMod, evasionMod, targetCount }`, `CombatDeedsPool`, `InjuryState`, and `CombatSim` — CombatEncounterState's fields (current HP/positions/statuses, consumed RNG cursor, which mob, sub-tick accumulator) are never enumerated anywhere. Separately, the intent is `{ type: 'combat_action'; action: CombatAction }` (line 3993) while §2.8(c) names the intervention type `CombatInterventionIntent` (line 1122) — two names for the mid-fight verb payload.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: CombatEncounterState is the one explicitly non-derivable, must-persist mid-fight blob (a save resumes the exact fight); leaving its shape unsketched is the highest-risk save/determinism type to under-specify. The CombatAction vs CombatInterventionIntent drift means the §6.3 contract and the §2.8 system sketch name the same payload differently.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Sketch CombatEncounterState's fields in §2.8(c)/§6.4 (combatants[], rngCursor, subTickAccumulator, activeStatuses, mobDefId, etc.) and unify the verb-payload name (use CombatAction everywhere, or note CombatAction = the inner action of CombatInterventionIntent).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified both halves against the source. (1) §2.8(c) lines 1115-1126 is the explicit "Rough DATA shape" sketch and lists Combatant, CombatSim, Stance, CombatInterventionIntent, CombatDeedsPool, InjuryState — but CombatEncounterState is never sketched, despite §6.4 line 4063 persisting `combat?: CombatEncounterState` as the explicitly non-derivable must-persist mid-fight blob (save resumes the exact fight) and M2a phase 1 line 4532 listing it as a core/combat export. Its fields (combatants/current HP, rng cursor, sub-tick accumulator, statuses, which mob) are enumerated nowhere. (2) The mid-fight verb payload is named CombatAction at §6.3 line 3993 vs CombatInterventionIntent at §2.8(c) line 1122 — and the scope also drifts: §6.3 has a separate set_stance intent (line 3991) so CombatAction = ability/item/retreat, while §2.8's CombatInterventionIntent bundles stance too. Genuine, NEW type-model gap; resolution needs design decisions, so needs-human not trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.question: CombatEncounterState is the one explicitly non-derivable, must-persist mid-fight blob (a save resumes the exact encounter), yet its fields are sketched nowhere (§2.8(c) skips it; §6.4 only gives a prose comment). (A) What fields does the persisted CombatEncounterState carry — e.g. combatants[] (current HP/statuses), rngCursor/stream position, subTickAccumulator, mobDefId, activeStatuses/effects? Sketch it in §2.8(c) and reference from §6.4. (B) The mid-fight verb payload is named two ways: CombatAction (§6.3 line 3993, scoped to ability/item/retreat, with a SEPARATE set_stance intent at line 3991) vs CombatInterventionIntent (§2.8(c) line 1122, scoped to stance/ability/item/retreat). Pick one canonical model: either (i) keep set_stance separate and narrow §2.8's name to CombatAction = {ability|item|retreat}, dropping stance from the intervention list; or (ii) fold stance back into a single CombatInterventionIntent and drop set_stance. Trade-off: (i) keeps stance as a normal pre/mid-fight intent consistent with §6.3's existing union; (ii) unifies all mid-fight verbs under one name but contradicts the already-listed set_stance intent.

confirmedFindings.title: RankId vs RungId: the DEV API's toRung helper uses an undeclared RungId while the rest of the model uses RankId
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.4 types the per-tier rung as `rung: RankId` and §6.5's ranks.ts registry is keyed by `RankId`. The DEV API declares `toRung(id: RungId): void` (line 4245). `RungId` appears nowhere else (grep: single hit) and is never defined; the canonical id for a rung/rank is `RankId`.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A second, undeclared name for the same id is dead type drift that fails strict tsc (unknown type) and confuses which id the helper takes.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Rename the parameter type to RankId.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md line 4245: the DEV API declares toRung(id: RungId): void. Grep shows RungId occurs exactly once in the file and is never defined, while the canonical id type for a rung is RankId — §6.4 line 4055 types the per-tier rung field as `rung: RankId` and §6.5 line 4102 keys the content/ranks.ts registry by `RankId`. This is dead, undeclared type drift (would fail strict tsc as an unknown type) and is the same id the helper should take. Not a restatement of any rounds-1-2 finding; not already reconciled. Clerical rename, no design judgement needed.

confirmedFindings.title: GameState.clock drops the lunarPhase and weather fields that §2.2 WorldClock declares; live weather has no persisted home
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.4 line 4044 vs §2.2(c) line 911 and §2.14(c) lines 1335-1338
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.2(c): `WorldClock { tick, day, season, year, lunarPhase, weather }`, and §2.14(c) 'Reuses WorldClock + Scheduler + SeasonalAppraisalState from 2.2'. §6.4 stores only `clock: { tick: number; day: number; season: Season; year: number }` — lunarPhase and weather are dropped. lunarPhase is a pure function of `day` (correctly derivable, fine to omit), but weather is a stochastic per-day draw (`WeatherHazard` from `SeasonRules.weatherWeights`) that modifies labour/combat rates; since active-only load resumes (no replay), the already-rolled current weather is non-derivable and must persist, yet has no field. (Note: SeasonalAppraisalState's `pendingAppraisalDue` likewise has no §6.4 home.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: On reload the current day's weather would either be lost or re-rolled — changing the live rate modifiers mid-day and advancing the RNG differently, breaking the byte-identical-resume guarantee.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either add `weather` (and document lunarPhase as derived) to GameState.clock, or explicitly state weather is recomputed deterministically and how that avoids RNG desync on resume.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed at the cited lines. §2.2(c) line 911 declares WorldClock with six fields (incl. lunarPhase, weather); §6.4 line 4044 persists clock with only four (tick/day/season/year), and §2.14(c) line 1339 says the world-sim reuses that WorldClock + SeasonalAppraisalState. weather (§2.14 line 1338) is a live stochastic draw that modifies labour/combat rates; §6.8 line 4182 guarantees active-only resume (no replay/catch-up) and §6.7 promises byte-identical replays. The PRD never states weather is recomputed deterministically, yet gives it no persisted home — so on reload the current day's weather is either lost or re-rolled (and re-rolling via the live split parent-counter, §6.7 line 4152, would advance the RNG differently and break determinism). lunarPhase is correctly omittable (pure function of day). Parenthetical also confirmed: SeasonalAppraisalState.pendingAppraisalDue has no §6.4 field (lastJudgedHighWaterMark plausibly maps to influence.highWater at line 4053). Real, new, unfixed gap between the §2.2 declared shape and the §6.4 persisted surface. It is a store-vs-recompute design decision with RNG-desync implications, hence needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">For the current day's weather (a per-day WeatherHazard draw that modifies live labour/combat rates), which model governs active-only resume? Option A — it is live state: add `weather` to GameState.clock in §6.4 (and note lunarPhase is derived from `day`), so reload restores the exact rolled hazard. Option B — it is recomputed on load: state in §2.2/§6.4 that weather is a pure function of (seed, day) via a day-keyed child RNG (not the live parent counter), so recomputation cannot perturb the main stream; then also document where pendingAppraisalDue lives. The trade-off: A keeps the save authoritative and matches the byte-identical-resume guarantee with no derivation subtlety, at the cost of one more persisted/migrated field; B keeps the save minimal but requires the weather sub-stream to be deterministically keyed by day (independent of how much the parent counter advanced mid-day) — otherwise reload re-rolls weather and breaks both the live rate modifiers and the seeded-RNG reproduction guarantee.
confirmedFindings.question: Is the current weather a stored live field (then add it to clock) or recomputed on resume — and if recomputed, how does that not perturb the seeded RNG / break byte-identical reload?

confirmedFindings.title: advance_dialogue references choiceId/ChoiceId, but the Dialogue/TextLine type declares no choices field
confirmedFindings.evidence: The intent is `{ type: 'advance_dialogue'; lineId: DialogueLineId; choiceId?: ChoiceId }` (line 3994), but §2.12(c) types a dialogue line as `Dialogue/TextLine { id, speaker, text, displayConditions (predicate), rewards: RewardBundle, locksLineIds[] }` — there is no `choices` / `choiceId` field on the line, and `ChoiceId` is referenced only here and never defined. So the verb accepts a choice id that no content type can produce or resolve.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Branching dialogue choices are an implied feature (the intent has a choiceId param) with no backing data shape — reduce() would have no way to validate or apply a choiceId, and the type is undeclared (fails strict tsc).
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a `choices?: { id: ChoiceId; text; rewards?; locksLineIds? }[]` field to the dialogue-line type in §2.12(c) and define `type ChoiceId`, or drop choiceId from the intent if branching is not a v1 verb.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed and genuinely new. §6.3 line 3994 declares the intent variant `{ type: 'advance_dialogue'; lineId: DialogueLineId; choiceId?: ChoiceId }`. A grep shows `ChoiceId` appears exactly once in the whole PRD (line 3994) and is never defined as a type. §2.12(c) line 1261 types the dialogue line as `Dialogue/TextLine { id, speaker, text, displayConditions, rewards: RewardBundle, locksLineIds[] }` — there is no `choices`/`choiceId` field, and §2.12(a) explicitly states branching is achieved by locking other lines (`locksLineIds[]`), not by intra-line choices. So the `choiceId` param is orphaned: no content shape can produce or resolve a choice id, and the type is undeclared. The context is a 'Rough DATA shape' / illustrative discriminated union (other Id types like ActivityId/AreaId are likewise not all formally defined here), which keeps severity low, but the param implies an intra-line-branching feature that the data model does not back. Resolving it is a design call (support intra-line choices vs. branch only via line-locking), not clerical, so needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Does v1 dialogue support intra-line branching choices, or is all branching done by line-locking (`locksLineIds[]`)? Trade-off: (A) Keep `choiceId` and add a backing shape — add `choices?: { id: ChoiceId; text; rewards?: RewardBundle; locksLineIds? }[]` to the §2.12(c) dialogue-line type and define `type ChoiceId`; this enables player-selectable in-line options but adds reducer/validation surface and content-authoring cost. (B) Drop `choiceId?: ChoiceId` from the `advance_dialogue` intent and rely solely on `locksLineIds[]` for branching (matches the §2.12(a) description); simpler, no new type, but no intra-line player choice. Which is canon for v1?</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Does v1 support intra-line dialogue choices? If so, add a choices[] field (and ChoiceId) to the dialogue-line type; if not, remove choiceId from the advance_dialogue intent.

confirmedFindings.title: Weather rate-modifiers and festival economic effects are promised v1 (T1) world-sim numbers but §4 delivers ZERO of them, and §4.7.1's yield formula has no weather term
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §4.7.1 line 2837 (yield formula); §2.14(c) lines 1335-1338; §6.4 line 4019; §6.7 lines 4150-4152; §7 M5 line 4625
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4 claims (preamble, line 2247) it is 'where the parked balance numbers from §§1–2 finally land.' §2.14 ships weather + festivals in T1/v1 (line 1346 'T1 brings seasons/weather/festivals to life') and types them as live mechanics: `WeatherHazard { kind, rateModifiers (labour/combat) }` (line 1338) and `Festival { ... effects (vendorRestock | socialBeat | reputationOpportunity) }` (line 1336). Weather is wired into the core type (`WorldClock {... weather }`, line 911), the §6.4 scheduler ('weather/festival events', line 4019), and even reserves its own RNG sub-stream ('a fight's rolls vs. weather', line 4152); M5 (line 4625) lists the 'festival/weather social layer' as deliverable content. Yet a grep of §4.0-§4.9 returns ZERO occurrences of 'weather' or 'festival'. Critically, the ONLY labour formula weather would modify — §4.7.1 line 2837 `yieldPerAction = baseYield · (1 + 0.04·skillLevel) · toolMult · seasonMult · (soft-stamina rate)` — has a `seasonMult` term but NO weather term, and no combat formula in §4.6 carries a weather modifier either. So a promised, type-backed, RNG-backed, milestone-scheduled v1 mechanic has no magnitudes and no formula hook anywhere in the number spine.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: M5 cannot implement weather's stated labour/combat rate effect (the magnitudes do not exist), and the §4.7.1/§4.6 formulas have no slot to put it — so either weather is silently dropped from v1 (contradicting §2.14e and M5) or it is implemented with invented numbers outside the balance model, defeating the single-source-of-truth contract.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either (a) add a `weatherMult` term to the §4.7.1 yield formula (and a combat rate modifier to §4.6) plus a small proposed weather-modifier table (e.g. cold/wet → ×0.9 labour, ×0.95 combat-speed) and a festival-effect table (vendorRestock %, reputation-opportunity ip) to §4.7/§4.8; or (b) explicitly park weather/festival *effects* to a named post-v1 milestone and downgrade §2.14e/M5 to 'cosmetic seasonal tags only in v1'.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: All cited facts verified in docs/prd.md. §2.14(c) types weather as a live mechanic with `rateModifiers (labour/combat)` (line 1338) and festivals with `effects (vendorRestock | socialBeat | reputationOpportunity)` (line 1336); §2.14(e) line 1346 brings weather/festivals "to life" at T1 (v1); §6.4 (line 4019) schedules weather/festival events; §6.7 (lines 4150-4152) reserves a weather RNG sub-stream; M5 (line 4625) lists the festival/weather social layer as a deliverable. §4's preamble (line 2247) declares it the place "where the parked balance numbers from §§1–2 finally land." Yet a grep of §4.0–§4.9 returns ZERO occurrences of weather/festival; the §4.7.1 yield formula (line 2837) carries `seasonMult` but no weather term; §4.6 combat has no weather modifier; and the §4.9 levers index lists no weather/festival dial. Seasons themselves ARE numerically covered (seasonMult, season-gate column, autumn ×3, the §4.2.2 seasonal-judged formula), so this is specifically a gap on weather rate-modifiers and festival economic effects, NOT a duplicate of the F2/F4 seasonal-JUDGE_K reconciliation. Genuine single-source-of-truth gap that blocks clean M5 implementation. Severity medium (not high): weather is explicitly "soft, never hard-blocking" flavor and festival socialBeat is narrative, so the load-bearing resource/Influence spine is unaffected — but concrete magnitudes (weather labour/combat rate mult, festival vendorRestock %/reputation ip) are still owed for an M5 deliverable. Not trivial-apply: assigning the missing magnitudes or parking the mechanic is a balance/design decision.
confirmedFindings.question: For v1, do weather hazards and festivals carry MECHANICAL effects that need magnitudes in §4, or are they cosmetic-only with effects parked post-v1? Options: (a) Mechanical — add a `weatherMult` term to the §4.7.1 yield formula and a combat-speed/rate modifier to §4.6, plus proposed tables: weather modifiers (e.g. cold/wet → ×0.9 labour, ×0.95 combat-speed; clear → ×1.0) in §4.7, and festival effects (vendorRestock %, reputation-opportunity ip) in §4.8, with a corresponding lever entry in §4.9. (b) Cosmetic-in-v1 — explicitly downgrade §2.14(e)/M5 to "seasonal/weather tags + festival social beats are flavour only in v1" and park the labour/combat/economic modifiers to a named post-v1 milestone, so §4 owes no magnitudes. Trade-off: (a) honors the "world nudges what's worth doing" pillar and keeps the SSOT contract intact but adds tuning surface and a small win-rate/throughput perturbation that must stay inside the locked 20–35% first-fight and ≥30-min-floor envelopes; (b) is cheaper and protects the locked pacing math but makes T1 weather/festivals presentational only, weakening the "living world" promise. If (a), what modifier magnitudes (and should weather multipliers be bounded, e.g. within ±10%, so they cannot push first-fight win-rate or per-rung throughput outside the locked bands)?

confirmedFindings.title: §4.9 levers index (the 'complete tuning dashboard') omits the entire §4.3 conversion-weight coefficient set
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.9 bills itself as 'Levers index (the tuning dashboard)' enumerating every tunable, and §4 preamble (line 2233) says 'every value lives in core/content/balance.ts (the single home for tunables).' §4.3 defines six pillar-conversion-weight coefficients used to scale every deed base — `1.0 + 0.05·combatLevel + 0.10·dangerRingClearedAbove` (line 2621), `0.04·skillLevel` (2622), `0.04·tradeSkill` (2623), `0.06·officeRank + 0.15·allianceSealed` (2624), `0.25·(...)/TIER_REF_NAME` (2625) — and §4.3 itself declares them levers ('Levers: the per-domain coefficients (the 0.04–0.25 multipliers)', line 2637). But §4.9 never references §4.3 or these coefficients at all; its 'Attributes: all coefficients (§4.4)' line covers §4.4, not §4.3. (This is the same class of omission round 2 found for the soft-stamina rate, which is likewise used in §4.7.1 and absent from the index.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A balance engineer tuning from the §4.9 dashboard will not see that deed→pillar conversion can be re-weighted at all, so these six dials — which directly govern how rewarding each domain feels to specialise in — are invisible to the documented tuning surface.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a 'Conversion weights (§4.3)' entry to the §4.9 index listing the six per-domain multipliers.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed: §4.9 "Levers index (the tuning dashboard)" (lines 3047-3069) enumerates Master dials, Accrual, Gating, Skills, Attributes (§4.4), Combat, Pacing, and Producers/costs levers, but never references §4.3 or its per-domain conversion-weight coefficients. A targeted grep of the §4.9 block found no match for §4.3, conversion, combatLevel, dangerRing, tradeSkill, officeRank, or allianceSealed (the only 0.25 hit is the unrelated PER_EVENT_XP_CAP_FRACTION). §4.3 itself explicitly declares these per-domain coefficients (the 0.04–0.25 multipliers) as "Levers" at line 2637, so their omission from the levers-index dashboard is a real completeness gap. It is genuinely new — distinct from the round-2 soft-stamina-rate omission (a different lever set). Severity low (documentation completeness, not a numeric error); the deed-base table they multiply is itself referenced (§4.2.1) but the §4.3 multipliers are a separate lever family. Fix is clerical: add an index entry listing the existing §4.3 coefficients verbatim, no design judgement.
confirmedFindings.new_string: **Conversion weights (§4.3):** the per-domain deed-base multipliers (`0.05·combatLevel`, `0.10·dangerRing`, `0.04·skillLevel`, `0.04·tradeSkill`, `0.06·officeRank`, `0.15·allianceSealed`, `0.25·Name-blend`). **Attributes:** all coefficients (§4.4), start = 5, +1 pt / 2 levels

confirmedFindings.title: §4.9 levers index omits the §4.6.1 combat-model base constants (hpMax base/per-level, skillBonus, accuracy base, crit base)
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §4.9 line 3065 (Combat lever line) vs §4.6.1 lines 2758-2766
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.6.1 introduces global combat constants that are NOT in the §4.4 attribute table and NOT in the §4.9 index: `skillBonus = 0.3·weaponSkillLevel` (line 2758), `accuracy = 10 + ...` (base 10, line 2761), `critChance = 0.02 + ...` (base 0.02, line 2763), and `hpMax = 40 + 8·characterLevel + 2·STR` (base 40 and the per-level coefficient 8, line 2765). The §4.9 'Combat:' line (3064-3066) lists only `baseSpeed`, `SPD coeff 0.005`, `DAMAGE_FLOOR`, `CRIT_MULT = 1.5`, `BLOCK_REDUCTION = 0.5`, `COMBAT_XP_K = 12`. The hpMax base/per-level term is especially load-bearing because the wolf win-rate calibration (§4.6.6) depends on it, yet neither it nor the 8·characterLevel slope appears in the tuning dashboard.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The single most calibration-sensitive combat numbers (base HP, HP-per-level, weapon-skill damage slope) are absent from the index that the §7 balance-pass milestone (M7.2) is instructed to tune 'by tuning the §4.9 levers only' — so they fall outside the documented tuning surface.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Append the missing §4.6.1 base constants to the §4.9 Combat lever line.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md. The §4.9 Combat lever line (lines 3064-3066) lists only baseSpeed, SPD coeff 0.005, DAMAGE_FLOOR, CRIT_MULT=1.5, BLOCK_REDUCTION=0.5, COMBAT_XP_K=12. The §4.6.1 combat-model base constants are absent: hpMax base 40 and the 8·characterLevel per-level slope (line 2765), skillBonus=0.3·weaponSkillLevel (line 2758), accuracy base 10 (line 2761), and crit base 0.02 (line 2763). Refutation failed: §4.4's attribute table (and §4.9's 'Attributes: all coefficients (§4.4)' reference) only covers the per-attribute coefficients (2·STR, 0.4·AGI, 0.2%·AGI, etc.) — NOT the level-based base/slope terms (40, 8·characterLevel) nor the weapon-skill slopes, which are not attribute coefficients. §4.6.1 carries no 'Lever:' callout of its own (unlike §4.6.2/4/5), so these constants are tagged tunable nowhere. M6.2 (line 4696) explicitly mandates tuning 'by tuning the §4.9 levers only,' and the wolf win-rate calibration (§4.6.6) depends on hpMax base 40 / 8·characterLevel — so genuinely load-bearing numbers sit outside the documented tuning surface. Real, low-severity completeness gap. Fix is a clerical cross-reference append (no value judgment), hence trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.new_string: `CRIT_MULT = 1.5`, `BLOCK_REDUCTION = 0.5`, `COMBAT_XP_K = 12`, `hpMax` base 40 + per-level 8·characterLevel, `skillBonus = 0.3·weaponSkillLevel`, accuracy base 10, crit base 0.02, crit/block chance caps 0.50/0.40 (all §4.6.1/§4.6.4);

confirmedFindings.title: Near-collision: T3 antagonist "Tedai Kuroiwa" vs the protagonist house "Kurosawa"
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §1.8 line 548; §1.11 line 639; §5 T3.3 lines 3690-3702 (Kuroiwa: 12 uses); "Kurosawa" used 54× throughout
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.8 L548: "**Tedai Kuroiwa** | The magistrate's agent (*tedai*) — the gracious facilitator who *records* your achievements." The house is "the **Kurosawa**" everywhere (L185 "The **Kurosawa** hold a modest hill estate"). In all T3 material both appear together (e.g. §5 T3.3 repeatedly says "Kuroiwa" while the tier-wide subject is "the Kurosawa"). "Kuroiwa" and "Kurosawa" share the "Kuro-" stem and differ only by "-iwa" vs "-sawa" — the single most-confusable pair in the doc since both are major, recurring, and co-located in T3 scenes/log lines.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: In a text-only woodblock UI where the player scans names in dense event-log lines, "Kuroiwa records the Kurosawa's yield" is genuinely mis-parseable. "Kurosawa" is LOCKED (the central house); the collision must be resolved by renaming the antagonist. The round-1 pass caught the milder Mago/Magobei pair but missed this central-house-vs-antagonist clash.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Rename the T3 tedai antagonist away from the "Kuro-" stem (e.g. a non-Kuro surname) so it never shares an initial cluster with the protagonist house. Kuroiwa is not on the locked-names list, so it is the side that should move.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: The quoted text exists verbatim (L548, L639, L3690): the T3 antagonist is "Tedai Kuroiwa" and the protagonist house is the "Kurosawa" (50 uses, locked canon). They share the "Kuro-" stem and co-occur throughout T3, and the antagonist's defining action is recording the house's yields, so a confusable string ("Kuroiwa records the Kurosawa's yield") is plausible in a text-dense log UI. This is a genuinely new pair, distinct from the round-1 Mago/Magobei catch, and not reconciled elsewhere. Severity dialed down to low: "Kuro" is a ubiquitous, authentic Japanese surname element, the two names differ by a full lexical element (-sawa marsh vs -iwa rock) and are never abbreviated to a bare "Kuro-", so competent readers distinguish them. The finding also misstates that "Kuroiwa is not on the locked-names list" — it IS in brainstorms/2026-06-25-locked-decisions.md L121, so renaming touches canon and is a human design decision, not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: The T3 antagonist "Tedai Kuroiwa" and the central protagonist house "Kurosawa" share the "Kuro-" stem, recur together in T3 scenes/log lines, and the antagonist's signature act is recording the house's yields — so a co-located line is mildly confusable in the text-only UI. Both names appear in locked canon (Kurosawa as the house; Kuroiwa in the locked-decisions antagonist roster). Options: (a) rename the T3 antagonist to a non-Kuro surname to fully remove the stem collision — cleanest, but edits canon and ~10 PRD uses; (b) keep both as authentic, distinct surnames (the -sawa/-iwa difference is clear and "Kuro-" is a common real-world prefix) and accept the minor overlap; (c) keep the name but ensure UI/log copy never juxtaposes the two bare surnames in one line. Which do you want?

confirmedFindings.title: Near-collision: village legend-women "Sayo" and "Obaa Sato" (both folklore-central, near-identical names)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.8 L518: "**Sayo** | The headman's daughter, ~16 … names him \"Tama\" on sight … the **living heart of the legend**." L519: "**Obaa Sato** | Village herbalist / wise-woman … the village's **folklore-keeper** — narrates the *kamikakushi* legend." Both are Asagiri women whose entire function is the Tama/kamikakushi legend; their bare names "Sayo" / "Sato" are two syllables, share the "Sa-" onset and "-o" coda, and differ by one medial consonant (y/t). In dialogue/log lines they appear as "Sayo" and "Sato".
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Two characters with one-letter-apart names occupying the same thematic role (the legend) in the same faction is a real reader/screen-reader confusion, exactly the orphan/near-collision class round-1/2 were asked to surface. "Obaa" (grandmother) is a descriptor, so the distinguishing token is just "Sato" vs "Sayo".
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Rename one (most cheaply the herbalist; "Obaa Sato"→a clearly distinct name, e.g. a non-Sa- name) so the two legend-women never near-rhyme in event-log copy.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Quoted text is verbatim-accurate: §1.8 L518 "Sayo … living heart of the legend" and L519 "Obaa Sato … folklore-keeper", echoed at §5 L3354/L3359. The two names (Sayo/Sato) are one medial consonant apart, share the Sa-/-o frame, and both occupy legend-adjacent roles in the same Asagiri faction — a genuine near-collision per the naming-concordance lens. However, severity is overstated as medium: grep confirms "Obaa Sato" ALWAYS carries the honorific descriptor (never bare "Sato"), so in copy it reads "Obaa Sato" vs "Sayo"; the two also differ sharply in age (16 vs elderly) and function (personal legend-ignition vs folklore-narration), and rarely co-occur. The collision is real but mild — a low-severity authorial naming nit, not a structural ambiguity. Appears new (rounds 1-2 collision work is documented at L533 renaming "Kuranosuke"→"Jinpachi", a different pair). Renaming a cast member is a design/authorial call, hence needs-human.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Sayo (headman's daughter, ~16, "living heart of the legend," names the MC "Tama" on sight) and Obaa Sato (elderly herbalist, "folklore-keeper" narrating the kamikakushi legend) are both legend-central Asagiri women whose bare names differ by one consonant (Sayo/Sato). The honorific "Obaa" always disambiguates in copy and their ages/functions differ sharply, so confusion risk in play is low. Do you want to rename one anyway (cheapest: give the herbalist a non-"Sa-" name) to remove the near-rhyme entirely, or accept it as adequately disambiguated by the "Obaa" honorific and leave the cast as-is?
confirmedFindings.question: Sayo (headman's daughter, legend's heart) and Obaa Sato (herbalist, folklore-keeper) differ by one letter and share the same thematic role. Rename one (the herbalist is the lower-cost change) to remove the near-rhyme?

confirmedFindings.title: Kanji/romaji mismatch: AGI assigned 体 ("body"), not an agility glyph
confirmedFindings.evidence: L2654: "| **AGI** (体) | `evasion += 0.6·AGI`; `critChance += 0.2%·AGI`; hit-accuracy `+0.4·AGI` | gathering speed `+0.6%·AGI` (forage/fish); craft success `+0.3%·AGI` |". The glyph 体 reads *tai/karada* = "body/physique" (a constitution stat), but AGI = "agility" and its listed effects are all dexterity/finesse (evasion, crit, accuracy, gather speed, craft success). HP/constitution is already under STR (力, `hpMax += 2·STR`). The other four are correct: STR 力, INT 智, SPD 速, LUCK 運. 体 is the lone glyph that does not match its romaji.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The prompt's explicit kanji-concordance check (力/体/智/速). Per the no-AI-slop/authenticity bar, a "body" kanji labelling an agility stat is a visible mistranslation in a kanji-forward UI. The conventional glyph for an agility stat is 敏 (bin, "nimble/quick"); 技 (skill) is the alternative.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Change 体 → 敏 (or 技) so the glyph matches AGI's semantics. Single occurrence, single-line edit.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md L2654: AGI is glyphed 体 (tai/karada = "body/physique", a constitution semantic). The other four glyphs match their romaji (STR 力 power, INT 智 wisdom, SPD 速 fast, LUCK 運 fortune), and AGI's own listed effects are all finesse/dexterity (evasion, crit, accuracy, gathering speed, craft success) while HP/constitution is already assigned to STR (hpMax += 2·STR). 体 is therefore the lone glyph mismatched to its stat. It is a single occurrence; the deferred-kanji ADR item (decisions.md L145) covers only "Standing & Office kanji," not attribute glyphs, so this is unreconciled and not a rounds-1-2 restatement. Real but low severity (cosmetic, kanji-forward authenticity). Picking the replacement glyph (敏 nimble / 技 skill / other) is an aesthetic-design call, not a clerical fix, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: AGI is currently glyphed 体 ("body/physique"), which reads as a constitution stat, yet all its effects are dexterity/finesse and HP already sits under STR. Replace the glyph to match AGI's semantics? Options: (a) 敏 (bin, "nimble/agile") — closest direct match for agility; (b) 技 (waza, "skill/technique") — emphasizes finesse/craft, fits the craft-success effect; (c) keep 体 if a deliberate "physique/athleticism" framing was intended (note this conflicts with STR owning HP/carry/durability). Recommended: 敏. Trade-off: 速 is already taken by SPD, so AGI's glyph must differentiate agility from raw speed — 敏 does this cleanly.

confirmedFindings.title: §1.8 cast roster omits several named NPCs that the narrative actually uses (Tōkichi, Konoe, et al.)
confirmedFindings.evidence: Named, used NPCs absent from the §1.8 cast tables: T0 personification **Moneylender Tōkichi** (defined only in §1.11 L636 and §5 T0.3 L3169-3170, yet a v1-active T0 figure); the T4 through-line face **rusui Konoe** ("the single recurring T4 through-line face", §5 T4.4 L3802; used 10×, in §1.7.1/§1.11 but not the §1.8 region cast L541-552); and §5-only T3/T4 NPCs **Proprietress Okatsu** (L3716), **Retainer Tadahiro** (L3717), **Go-between Omiya-no-Sahei** (L1497/L3718), **Town-agent Heikuro** (L3708), inspector's secretary **Mabuchi** (L3797). §1.8 claims to be the cast "by faction & area" with a "First appears" column.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: §1.8 is the single concordance anchor ("cross-ref §1.8" is cited from §5 rosters). Characters defined only in narrative prose have no canonical role/age/faction row, so the "single source of truth" for cast is incomplete — Tōkichi especially, since he is v1 T0 content, not parked.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add at least Tōkichi (T0) and Konoe (T4 through-line) to §1.8 (a neutral/external group for Tōkichi; the region/Edo group for Konoe), and either list or explicitly defer the T3/T4 minor faces; the table already tags parked entries "(later)".
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Facts verified against docs/prd.md. The §1.8 cast tables (L486-552) do omit all named NPCs cited: Tōkichi (L636, L3169-70), Konoe (used ~10x incl. L470/668/2174/3802), and the §5-only T3/T4 faces Okatsu/Tadahiro/Omiya-no-Sahei/Heikuro/Mabuchi (L3708-18, 3797). §5 rosters explicitly cite "cross-ref §1.8" as the concordance anchor (e.g. L3187, L3565, L3586), so the table is meant to be the single source of truth for cast. The partial refutation holds for Tōkichi (explicitly "a circumstance, not a conspirator," deliberately cut even from the T0.4 roster list) and for the §5-only minor faces (§1.8 self-scopes its Region group as "T2+, mostly parked" and the estate roster as growing per tier). But Konoe survives refutation: he is a recurring, named, load-bearing T4 spine figure (the MC's sole capital proxy) with no canonical §1.8 row, while Echizen-ya Sōbei — the apex factor Konoe forwards dispatches to, and a deeper-T4 figure — IS listed (L552). That is a genuine, if minor, gap in the cited anchor table. Severity low (documentation completeness only, no gameplay/logic impact). Fix is a scoping judgment, not clerical, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: §1.8 is cited by §5 rosters as the cast concordance anchor, yet it omits rusui Konoe — a recurring, named T4 spine figure (the MC's sole capital proxy, used ~10x) — even though the apex Edo factor he serves, Echizen-ya Sōbei, does get a row. Should §1.8 add a Region/Edo row for Konoe (and adopt an explicit inclusion rule), choosing among: (a) add Konoe only, since he is a recurring spine conduit, and leave Tōkichi/the §5-only T3/T4 faces out as intentional personification/parked deferrals; (b) also add the §5-only minor faces (Okatsu, Tadahiro, Omiya-no-Sahei, Heikuro, Mabuchi) as parked "(later)" rows for full concordance; or (c) leave §1.8 as-is and instead add a one-line scope note stating that §1.8 excludes personifications (Tōkichi) and forward T3/T4 narrative-only NPCs, which live in §5? Trade-off: (a)/(b) make the anchor truly complete at the cost of table bulk and pre-committing forward cast; (c) is cheapest but means "cross-ref §1.8" remains a partial index that readers must supplement with §5.

confirmedFindings.title: "Nao-" front-collision (heir Naoyuki vs clerk Naozane) plus a wider rhyming-name cluster
confirmedFindings.evidence: Naoyuki (Kurosawa heir, 23×, §1.8 L497) and Naozane (guilt-sick T3 clerk, 6×, §1.8 L550) share the distinctive "Nao-" onset and both surface in T3 (§5 T3.5 L3719-3720 names "Naoyuki" and "Clerk Naozane" in adjacent clauses). Adjacent rhyming pairs also exist: rival heir **Kageyuki** vs Kurosawa heir **Naoyuki** (both -yuki, both "the heir", co-located in T2 §5 L3528-3539); **Sōzaemon** (Tomita head) vs **Sōbei** (Edo factor) (both Sō-, both antagonist-side); **Kanta** (origin friend) vs **Kanbei** (jealous retainer) (both Kan-).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Word-initial similarity is what readers/screen-readers use to disambiguate fast; Naoyuki/Naozane is the worst because both are story-important and appear together in T3. The -emon suffix cluster (Genemon/Yagōemon/Tokuemon/Saburozaemon/Sōzaemon) is period-authentic and acceptable, but stacking front-collisions on top raises cognitive load.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: At minimum rename the T3 clerk off "Nao-" (Naozane→a non-Nao name) to separate him from the heir; optionally nudge one of Kageyuki/Naoyuki off the shared -yuki. Leave the authentic -emon cluster as-is.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Verified against docs/prd.md: heir Kurosawa Naoyuki (L497, LOCKED) and guilt-sick T3 clerk Naozane (L550) share the distinctive "Nao-" onset and both appear in the T3 narrative (§5: Naozane L3694/3716, Naoyuki-as-Tomita-ally L3719). Rival heir Kageyuki and Kurosawa heir Naoyuki both end in -yuki and co-occur in T2 §5 (L3528-3539, both framed as "heir"). Sōzaemon/Sōbei (both Sō-, antagonist-side, L546/L552) and Kanta/Kanbei (both Kan-, L537/L511) also confirmed. The finding is factually accurate and NOT a restatement of the -emon cluster it explicitly concedes as acceptable period flavour. It is consistent with the authors' own demonstrated standard: the PRD already deliberately separated Otsuru from sweetheart Osen "to avoid confusion" (L539) and renamed Jinpachi off the "colliding Kuranosuke" (L533). The strongest sub-claim (Naoyuki/Naozane front-collision, both important, co-occurring in T3) is a genuine low-severity readability concern. It is needs-human, not trivial-apply: the candidate renames touch LOCKED canon (Naoyuki-as-heir; Tomita lineage incl. Kageyuki/Sōzaemon) and are a naming/taste judgment, not a clerical fix.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Two story-important names share a distinctive onset and co-occur in T3: heir Kurosawa Naoyuki (LOCKED) and the guilt-sick clerk Naozane. Should the clerk be renamed off "Nao-" to reduce read/screen-reader confusion (Naozane is not marked LOCKED, so this is low-cost)? Separately, the LOCKED canon has two rhyming clusters — rival heir Kageyuki vs Kurosawa heir Naoyuki (both -yuki, both "heir"), and Tomita head Sōzaemon vs Edo factor Sōbei (both Sō-, both antagonist-side). Options: (a) rename only the non-locked clerk Naozane (minimal, removes the worst collision); (b) also nudge one of Kageyuki/Naoyuki off -yuki (touches LOCKED Tomita lineage / heir name — higher cost, needs canon re-confirmation); (c) accept all as period-authentic flavour and leave as-is. Trade-off: front-initial similarity is what readers use to disambiguate fast, but every change to Naoyuki/Kageyuki/Sōzaemon reopens a LOCKED decision. The deliberately-period -emon cluster (Genemon/Yagōemon/Tokuemon/Saburozaemon/Sōzaemon) should be left untouched regardless.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Heir Naoyuki and clerk Naozane share the "Nao-" onset and co-occur in T3. Rename the clerk off "Nao-"? And do the rival-heir rhyme (Kageyuki/Naoyuki) and Sōzaemon/Sōbei need separation, or is that acceptable flavour?

confirmedFindings.title: M1 BLOCKER: the Estate Service / Combat Standing rank-meters have no accrual rule and no per-rung crossing thresholds — the entire T0 spine has no codeable rung-flip condition
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.15(c) line 1402; §6.4 line 4055; §4.8.1 lines 2955-2961; §7.2 M1 phase 4 line 4503
confirmedFindings.evidence: The estate spine flips rungs on two meters: §2.15(c) (1402) declares only a SHAPE — "Two meters: `EstateService` (steep geometric), `CombatStanding`." §6.4 (4055) stores them as bare numbers — "ranks: Record<TierId, { estateService: number; combatStanding: number; rung: RankId }>" — but NOTHING in §4 says how `estateService` increments per labour action/deed, nor what value flips R0→R1, R1→R2, etc. §4.8.1 writes only crossing-notation, e.g. R1 leaves at "~750 koku produced; ~3 Estate deeds (first yields); Estate Service x1" and R2 at "...Estate Service x2" — "x1/x2" are crossing tallies, not meter thresholds, and the relationship between the koku/deed counts and the separate `estateService` meter is never stated. Yet M1 phase 4 (4503) requires: "the Estate Service standing meter (§2.15) accrues from recognized labour and crosses the R0→R1→R2 rung gates ... Vitest asserts each rung flips at the intended GameState." You cannot write the accrual function, the rung-flip predicate, or that test from the spec.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Rung promotions are the dominant UI-reveal driver and the spine of T0; without a numeric gate the very first content milestone has no implementable or testable advance condition.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Define, in §4, the EstateService/CombatStanding accrual formula (what increments the meter and by how much per recognized labour action / deed) and a numeric threshold per rung (the value at which R0→R1, R1→R2, ... fire), OR explicitly collapse the rung gate onto the already-quantified koku-produced + deed-count targets in §4.8.1 and delete the separate meter as a stored field. Until one of these is chosen, M1 cannot be started.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against source. The stored fields ranks.estateService/combatStanding (§6.4 line 4055) are bare numbers, §2.15(c) line 1402 specifies only a curve SHAPE ("steep geometric"), and §2.15(c) line 1401 earnedBy references "EstateService|CombatStanding thresholds + trustBeats" — yet no section gives (a) an accrual rule (what increments the meter per labour action/deed and by how much) or (b) the numeric threshold that fires each rung crossing. §4.1/§4.2 quantify only the four Influence PILLARS (Arms/Estate/Office/Name), a different system from the two rank-meters. §4.8.1's "Estate Service x1...x5" are wall-clock crossing tallies, not meter thresholds (confirmed). M1 phase 4 (line 4503) nonetheless demands the meter "accrues from recognized labour," "crosses the R0→R1→R2 rung gates," and that "Vitest asserts each rung flips at the intended GameState" — which is unwritable from the spec. The gap is genuine and a model ambiguity (numeric meter vs. event/flag-gated rungs with a cosmetic meter) only a human can resolve. Downgraded from critical to high because the finding overstates "entire T0 spine has no codeable condition": §3.2.1 (lines 1833-1841) supplies concrete narrative triggers per rung (spilled-rice task complete, surviving the wolf, returning the ledger) that ARE codeable as flags/events, so R0→R1 is implementable today and M1 is only partly blocked — the missing piece is the meter accrual law plus the few numeric thresholds the prose references (R1→R2 "Estate-Service output threshold", R4→R5 "Combat-Standing threshold"). Needs-human, not trivial-apply: choosing the model changes stored state and the M1 tests.
confirmedFindings.question: How is an estate-ladder rung promotion gated — pick ONE model, because each stores different GameState and needs different M1 tests? OPTION A (numeric meter): rungs flip when a stored estateService/combatStanding meter crosses a threshold. Then §4 must add (1) the accrual rule — what increments the meter (recognized labour actions? recorded yields? koku produced?) and by how much per event, on the "steep geometric" curve §2.15(c) names — and (2) a per-rung threshold table (the value at which R0→R1, R1→R2, ... fire for T0, and the analogous V/G crossings). Trade-off: matches the §6.4 stored-number shape and §2.15 "thresholds" language, but introduces a third quantified progression curve to balance alongside koku and the four pillars. OPTION B (event/flag-gated, meter cosmetic): rungs flip on the §3.2.1 narrative triggers (spilled-rice task complete, a season of labour elapsed, surviving the wolf, returning the ledger) expressed as flags/counts already in GameState; estateService/combatStanding become derived display values (e.g. count of rungs earned, or a smoothed progress bar) with no independent gating role. Then drop or redefine the stored estateService/combatStanding numbers, delete the "thresholds" wording in §2.15(c)'s earnedBy, and gate rungs on flags/counts/resources directly. Trade-off: nothing new to balance and M1 phase 4's "spilled-rice/first real work/season of reliable labour" descriptions map straight to flags, but you lose the single numeric "Estate-Service output threshold" the prose at §3.2.1 R1→R2 implies. Which model is canon?

confirmedFindings.title: M1 BLOCKER: the soft-stamina throttle function and the staminaCost / drain / rest-recovery numbers are explicitly deferred to §4 but never defined there
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §3.8 line 2188; §4.7.1 line 2837; §2.3(c) lines 940-941; §4.9 levers index lines 3047-3069; §7.2 M1 phases 2-3 lines 4501-4502
confirmedFindings.evidence: §3.8 (2188) defers it: "Numbers → §4 ... soft-stamina rate ... are deferred." The §4.7.1 yield formula (2837) then embeds the factor without ever defining it: "yieldPerAction = baseYield · (1 + 0.04·skillLevel) · toolMult · seasonMult · (soft-stamina rate)". §2.3(c) (940-941) gives only prose: "a soft-throttle function maps low satiety → a *rate multiplier* on labour/combat speed (never to zero)" — no function, no per-action `staminaCost` values, no satiety drain-per-action, no rest-recovery amount. The §4.9 levers index never lists a soft-stamina/satiety dial at all. Yet M1 phase 2 (4501) requires implementing the §4.7.1 formula INCLUDING "stamina-rate", and phase 3 (4502) requires "soft stamina/satiety drains on labour and soft-throttles the action rate (slows, never to zero, never hard-blocks) with a rest verb recovering it" plus a Vitest "stamina throttles-not-blocks" assertion.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The stamina rate is a multiplicative factor in every labour yield and the day-pacing mechanism; M1's core loop, yield numbers, and its 'slows-never-blocks' acceptance test all depend on a function that the spec promises but never delivers.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add to §4 a §4.7.x soft-stamina block: the throttle function (rate-multiplier as a function of current satiety, with its non-zero floor), the per-action staminaCost defaults per node, the per-action satiety drain, and the rest-verb recovery amount per tick. Register these as levers in §4.9.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source. §3.8 (line 2188) defers "soft-stamina rate" to §4; §4.7.1 (line 2837) embeds "(soft-stamina rate)" as a multiplicative factor in the yield formula but its "Levers:" line (2865-2866) never defines it; §2.3(c) (940-941) gives only prose ("a soft-throttle function maps low satiety → a rate multiplier...never to zero") with no function, no per-node staminaCost value, no per-action satiety drain, and no rest-recovery rate; and the §4.9 levers index (3047-3069, read in full) lists no soft-stamina/satiety dial at all. Meanwhile M1 phase 2 (4501) requires the §4.7.1 formula "including stamina-rate" and phase 3 (4502) requires drain + soft-throttle + rest-recovery with a Vitest assertion. So the quoted text exists and says what is claimed; the value is promised-but-never-delivered. Downgraded from high to medium: the throttle SHAPE ("slows, never to zero, never hard-blocks") is fully specified, the acceptance test only checks that shape (satisfiable with placeholder magnitudes), and macro pacing is explicitly folded into "net throughput (after stamina, food...)" in §4.7.1 — so this gates a faithful implementation and the four balance magnitudes, but does not hard-block M1's core loop the way a missing pacing-budget would.
confirmedFindings.question: Define the soft-stamina/satiety system's four missing magnitudes so M1 phases 2-3 can be built and the §4.7.1 yield formula's "(soft-stamina rate)" factor is resolved. Specifically: (1) the rate-multiplier curve — multiplier as a function of current satiety % (e.g. linear from 1.0 at full down to a floor, or a kinked curve that only bites below some threshold like 50%), and (2) its non-zero floor value (the prose says "never to zero" — e.g. 0.5? 0.3?); (3) the per-action satiety drain (flat per action, or scaled by the node's staminaCost field) and default staminaCost per labour node; (4) the rest-verb recovery amount per tick (and whether resting advances the clock at a fixed rate). Trade-off: a shallow curve with a high floor (e.g. floor 0.7, bites only below 40%) makes stamina a gentle background texture that rarely changes player behaviour; a steeper curve with a low floor (e.g. floor 0.3, bites below 60%) makes rest/eat a real recurring decision and gives cooking/food genuine weight — but risks feeling like the "punishing timer" the design explicitly forbids. Once chosen, these should be added as a §4.7.x block and registered in the §4.9 levers index. Recommend the gentle end given the "never a wall, paces the day" design intent, but the exact numbers are balance values only the human sets.

confirmedFindings.title: M2b BLOCKER: durability is stored and 'worn' but its wear rate, zero-durability/break behaviour, and stat effect are undefined
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.10(c) lines 1192-1196; §6.4 line 4052; §7.2 M2b phase 2 line 4559 and phase 6 line 4563
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.10(c) (1196) gives only "Durability { current, max, repairCost }" and (1186) the prose "durability that wears and is repaired/re-crafted". §6.4 (4052) stores per-slot "{ equipDefId; durability; qualityTier }". But the spec never says how MUCH durability is lost per swing/fight, what happens at durability 0 (does the item stop working, drop to base, become unequippable?), or whether an item's statMods scale with current durability. M2b phase 2 (4559) requires "reduce ... applies durability wear immutably" and phase 6 (4563) renders "equip slots with durability" — both need a wear/break rule that does not exist. (The known save-determinism-5 concerned WHERE durability is stored; that is now resolved — the open gap is the wear/break BEHAVIOUR.)
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Durability touches the equipment intents, the combat-stat selectors, the craft/repair economy, and the renderer; an undefined wear/break rule blocks the M2b equipment slice and risks a determinism-affecting ad-hoc implementation.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify in §4.7 (alongside the gear loop) the durability wear per combat hit/fight (and per labour action for tools), the behaviour at 0 durability, and whether stats degrade with durability or only break at 0. Add these as §4.9 levers.
