confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed against source. §2.10(c) (lines 1192-1196) defines durability data shape only (EquipDef.durabilityMax, EquipState.durability, Durability{current,max,repairCost}); §6.4 (line 4052) just persists the per-slot value; §2.10(b) (line 1186) gives only the prose "durability that wears and is repaired/re-crafted." Grep for durabil/wear/repair/break shows NO section defines: (1) how much durability is lost per hit/fight/labour action, (2) behaviour at 0 (item unusable vs. drops to base stats vs. auto-unequip), or (3) whether statMods scale with current durability or only cut out at break. The only §4 trace is line 2653 (STR "raises carry/durability damage"), which is cryptic and defines no rule. M2b phase 2 (line 4559) explicitly requires reduce to "apply durability wear immutably" inside the deterministic, no-Math.random core, and phase 6 (line 4563) renders "equip slots with durability" — both depend on a wear/break rule that does not exist. Distinct from the resolved save-determinism-5 (WHERE durability is stored); this is the wear/break BEHAVIOUR. The gap touches equip intents, combat-stat selectors, the craft/repair economy, and the renderer, and an ad-hoc fix would be determinism-affecting — so it is a real, design-level gap requiring a human decision, not a clerical default. Severity medium (scoped to the M2b equipment slice, not a core-loop blocker) and needs-human are both appropriate.
confirmedFindings.question: Define the durability wear/break model for equipment so M2b's deterministic reduce can apply it. Three coupled decisions: (1) WEAR RATE — fixed durability lost per combat hit taken/dealt and per fight, and per labour action for tools? (constant per action, or scaled by hit damage/enemy tier?). (2) AT-ZERO BEHAVIOUR — does a 0-durability item become unequippable (auto-unequip to empty slot), keep functioning at reduced/base stats, or stay equipped but contribute zero statMods until repaired? (auto-unequip is simplest and most legible; degrade-to-base is gentler but adds a stat branch). (3) STAT SCALING — do statMods scale continuously with current/max durability (e.g. linear falloff) or stay at full value until the item breaks at 0 (binary)? Trade-off: continuous scaling adds combat-feel depth but complicates the §4.6 effectiveStats fold and balance tuning; binary (full-until-break) is far simpler and matches the "clear power step per tier" framing in §2.10(b). Recommend documenting the mechanic in §2.10 (and the numbers as §4.9 levers), since §4 currently has no durability entries.

confirmedFindings.title: No bulk / sell-all / craft-batch affordance for trade and crafting — a near-universal genre convention both inspirations provide
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §2.4 lines 963-967, §2.11 lines 1217-1219; grep for sell-all/buy-max/batch/queue returns nothing
confirmedFindings.evidence: Trade is described as "convert surplus to coin via trade (brokers/shops)" with a "market-saturation damper [that] depresses sell prices when one good is flooded, recovering over in-game days" (§2.4 lines 963-967). Crafting is "gather inputs, craft a tool/item at a station" (§2.11 line 1217). With koku/material counts climbing into the tens-of-thousands→millions (§4.0 lines 2282-2286) and an explicit incentive to dump surplus, there is no 'sell all / sell max / sell to saturation' control, and crafting has no batch/queue/'craft x10'. Grep for `sell all|buy max|batch|queue|craft all|in bulk` finds only the unrelated `convert surplus` prose. YAIRPG (inspiration #2) offers stack operations and a queued/automatable action model; the genre baseline is one-click bulk transactions.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Selling thousands of koku/units or crafting trade-goods one click at a time directly amplifies the action-loop ergonomics problem (gap-1) on the very surfaces — trade and the silk meibutsu sub-engine — that the design wants players to engage with for the Estate & Wealth pillar. Genre players will perceive its absence as a missing basic affordance.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a bulk-transaction affordance to §2.4/§2.11: a 'sell N / sell all / sell to the saturation floor' control on the broker/shop UI and a quantity/'craft to materials' option on the craft station. Note that bulk selling must respect the saturation damper (selling a stack walks the price index down per unit), which is good — it makes the damper legible.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
The factual claim is correct: grep for sell-all/buy-max/batch/queue/craft-all returns nothing, and the cited prose (§2.4 963-967 "convert surplus to coin via trade"; §2.11 1217-1219 "gather inputs, craft a tool/item at a station") is verbatim accurate — the PRD specifies no bulk-transaction or craft-N/queue affordance. However, the medium-severity framing is overstated: (1) koku is the base score-like currency you grind/spend, not a stack you click-sell, so the 'sell thousands→millions of koku' premise (citing §4.0 2282-2288) misreads the model — bulk selling would apply only to trade goods/materials; (2) TRADE is a hard-capped ≤⅓-of-one-pillar strand (§4.2.3 2565-2592, ~8% of total Influence) that 'can never carry a tier gate,' so it is a deliberately minor surface where bulk ergonomics matter much less than in a trade-centric idle game; (3) the finding explicitly says it 'amplifies gap-1' (the action-loop ergonomics finding), so it partly extends an existing concern. The genuinely-new kernel that survives is design-substantive, not just UI: there is no craft-quantity affordance for the silk/processing chains and, more importantly, no stated rule for how the §2.11/§4 market-saturation damper applies across a bulk sale (per-unit progressive walk-down vs single pre-sale price). That damper-vs-bulk interaction is a real undecided balance question, which makes this a legitimate but low-severity, needs-human design point rather than a medium gap.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Should §2.4/§2.11 add a bulk affordance for trade and crafting — a 'sell N / sell-all / sell-to-saturation-floor' control on the broker/shop and a 'craft N / craft-to-materials' quantity option on craft stations — and, critically, how should the market-saturation damper apply to a bulk sale: progressively per-unit (walking the price index down across the stack, which makes the damper legible but requires defining the per-unit step) or once at the pre-sale price (simpler, but lets a single dump dodge the damper)? Trade-offs: (a) bulk controls are a near-universal genre convention and reduce click-friction on the silk meibutsu sub-engine, but (b) TRADE is a deliberately hard-capped ≤⅓ minor strand and the 'lean, no fluff' pillar resists feature bloat, and koku itself is not sold by the stack — so the payoff is limited to trade goods. Recommend at minimum deciding the damper-application rule (it is a balance invariant, §4.2.3/§4.7), and optionally noting a quantity stepper as an implementation-level UI nicety rather than a PRD-canon feature.
confirmedFindings.question: Should trade selling and crafting expose bulk controls (sell-all / sell-to-floor / craft-N), and should bulk selling apply the market-saturation damper progressively across the stack? This is standard genre UX but interacts with the saturation balance (§2.11/§4) and the 'lean, no fluff' pillar.

confirmedFindings.title: No achievements / career-stats surface, yet GameState.counts is documented as 'drive achievements' — a dangling feature reference and a genre-expected page that is absent
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.4 line 4061 (`counts` comment), §6.9 line 4203 (screen list), §2.18 banzuke
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.4 stores `counts: Record<CountId, number>; // kills, clears, harvests — drive achievements/quests` (line 4061) — the only use of 'achievements' as a *player feature*; everywhere else 'achievement' means the pillar-accrual 'achievement JUMPS'. No achievement registry, no achievement type/screen, and no career-stats/totals page exists anywhere. The renderer screen list is "Estate / Village / Wilds / Skills / Combat / Influence / Map / Journal / Settings" (line 4203) — no Stats or Achievements screen; the banzuke (§2.18) renders only the four-pillar house RANK, not lifetime totals (kills, harvests, days played). YAIRPG (inspiration #2) ships explicit Stats and Levels/unlocks tabs; a stats/achievements page is a standard idle-RPG retention surface.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Either line 4061 promises an achievements feature that the spec never builds (dangling reference that will confuse implementers), or 'achievements' is loose shorthand for achievement-jumps (in which case the genre-expected stats/achievements page is simply a gap). Tracked `counts` are collected but never surfaced to the player, wasting stored state and omitting a long-tail engagement hook the inspirations rely on.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide: (a) add a lightweight career-stats / records view (e.g. fold totals into the Journal screen) that reads `counts` and lifetime koku — diegetic as a household record-book; or (b) if no achievements feature is intended, reword line 4061's comment from 'drive achievements/quests' to 'drive achievement-JUMPS/quests' to kill the dangling reference.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed verbatim: docs/prd.md line 4061 comment says counts "drive achievements/quests", and a full grep shows "achievements" as a player-feature noun occurs ONLY here — every other "achievement" is the pillar "achievement JUMP" or an NPC recording deeds. The §6.9 screen list (line ~4203) has no Stats/Achievements screen, and no achievements registry/type exists. So the comment is a genuine dangling reference. But the medium/needs-human framing is overstated: v1 milestones (M0-M7) are frozen and never include an achievements feature anywhere, so the intent is unambiguously "loose shorthand," counts already feed quest-advance events and the discover-by-encounter bestiary (so the state is not wasted), and the fix is the clerical reword (option b), not a scope decision. Low severity, trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.old_string: counts: Record<CountId, number>;               // kills, clears, harvests — drive achievements/quests
confirmedFindings.new_string: counts: Record<CountId, number>;               // kills, clears, harvests — drive quest advancement & bestiary tallies (NOT a separate player "achievements" feature; pillar achievement-JUMPS are recognized deeds, §2.16, not these raw counts)
confirmedFindings.question: Is a player-facing achievements/career-stats surface (reading the tracked kill/clear/harvest counts + lifetime totals) in scope for v1, or is 'achievements' at §6.4 line 4061 just shorthand for the pillar 'achievement-jumps'? If the latter, the comment should be reworded to remove the dangling feature implication.

confirmedFindings.title: No in-game changelog / patch-notes surface — only an engineering commit-SHA stamp; both inspirations ship a changelog
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §7.2 M7 line 4722; grep for changelog/patch-note/what's-new returns only schemaVersion engineering uses
confirmedFindings.evidence: The only versioning surface is engineering provenance: the build "stamps the source commit SHA / version into the Settings/About surface so any shipped zip is traceable to the commit" (line 4722). Grep for `changelog|patch note|what's new` finds no player-facing changelog anywhere; all other 'version' hits are `schemaVersion`/save-migration internals. proto23 (inspiration #1) keeps a `changelog` folder and YAIRPG (inspiration #2) exposes a changelog link — a near-universal convention for a post-launch-updated idle/incremental game so returning players can see what changed.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A single-player itch.io game that ships balance/content updates (M6 retuning, parked-feature additions) gives returning players no in-game signal of what changed; a commit SHA is meaningless to players. Low severity (cosmetic / retention), but it is a conspicuous omission relative to both named inspirations.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Add a small player-facing changelog/'What's new' panel to the Settings/About surface (a short authored list per release), sourced from a `docs/`/data file so it generates rather than hand-duplicates. Can be parked to post-launch, but should be named so it isn't forgotten.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md: grep for changelog|patch.?note|what'?s.new|release.note returns ZERO hits across the whole PRD. Line 4722 confirms the sole versioning surface is engineering provenance ("stamps the source commit SHA / version into the Settings/About surface so any shipped zip is traceable to the commit"). The §7.1.3 PARKED-for-v1 list (lines 4405-4413) does not include a player-facing changelog, so it is neither implemented nor deliberately deferred — a genuine omission. The observation is accurate and new (not a rounds-1-2 restatement). Severity is correctly low/cosmetic-retention: the finding itself concedes it is parkable post-launch, and itch.io provides native devlogs as the conventional out-of-game channel, which weakens (but does not eliminate) the need for an in-game panel. Properly a human scope decision, not a clerical fix — no unique verbatim line to mechanically edit.
confirmedFindings.question: Should lean v1 add a small player-facing 'What's new'/changelog panel to the Settings/About surface (a short authored per-release list, sourced from a docs/ data file so it generates rather than hand-duplicates), matching both named inspirations? Trade-off: (a) Add it — gives returning players a signal for M6 balance retunes and parked-feature additions; low build cost; matches genre convention. (b) Rely on itch.io's native devlog feature instead — zero in-game build cost, conventional for itch-hosted games, but invisible to players already inside the running game. (c) Explicitly park post-launch and name it so it is not forgotten. Recommend (b) or (c) given the lean v1 discipline and the deliberate T3 'the story pauses here' stop point, unless a committed post-launch content-update cadence is planned.

confirmedFindings.title: The signature gate line surfaces the 'clean your room' design-meta as literal in-fiction narration
confirmedFindings.evidence: V7's player-facing log line reads: *"The room is clean — house, valley, all of it. The lord: 'Now, the region.' Two rival names surface from beyond the ridge: Tomita. Akagi."* — "clean your room" is a design shorthand used internally ~9 times (lines 421, 1462, 1944, 2317, 3275, etc.) as the gate label; here the metaphor leaks verbatim into the diegetic narration even though nothing was literally cleaned (the beat is 'estate healthy, valley anchored').
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Of every diegetic line in §3, this is the one capstone-of-a-tier beat the player reads at the T1→T2 climax. "The room is clean" reads as a modern self-help / productivity-meme metaphor ('clean your room' à la pop-psych), not grounded mid-Edo narration. The §1.2/§1.3 register is 'grounded, restrained Edo' and the other 23 log lines stay sensory and concrete; this one alone speaks in the design doc's own meta-voice.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Re-cast the V7 log line in concrete estate terms (the literal state the metaphor stands for) rather than echoing the internal 'clean your room' label.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md:1913: the V7 player-facing event-log line reads "The room is clean — house, valley, all of it. The lord: 'Now, the region.' Two rival names surface from beyond the ridge: Tomita. Akagi." Everywhere else (lines 421, 1462, 2317, 3275, 1938, 1944, etc.) "clean your room" appears explicitly *quoted* as internal design shorthand for the gate; even the gate-description column of row 1913 quotes it as meta. Only the diegetic log line renders the metaphor unquoted as literal narration. The 23 sibling diegetic lines (V0–V6, rows 1906–1912) are uniformly concrete/sensory, so this one is a genuine register outlier where the design doc's own meta-voice surfaces in fiction. NOT a restatement of the gate-label usage. Downgraded from claimed medium to low: it is a single subjective stylistic line in a design spec (not shipped copy), and the phrasing is at least arguably evocative leitmotif rather than a defect. Fix is a taste/creative judgment (is the leitmotif intended player-facing?), so needs-human, not trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.old_string: *"The room is clean — house, valley, all of it. The lord: 'Now, the region.' Two rival names surface from beyond the ridge: Tomita. Akagi."*
confirmedFindings.new_string: *"The kura is solvent, the roads held, the valley settled around the house. The lord: 'Now, the region.' Two names surface from beyond the ridge: Tomita. Akagi."*
confirmedFindings.question: The V7 (T1→T2 capstone) player-facing event-log line at docs/prd.md:1913 opens with "The room is clean — house, valley, all of it." — echoing the internal "clean your room" design-shorthand verbatim (which everywhere else in the PRD is quoted as a meta gate-label). Every sibling diegetic log line (V0–V6) is concrete/sensory grounded-Edo narration. Options: (A) Re-cast in concrete estate terms, e.g. "The kura is solvent, the roads are held, the valley settles around the house. The lord: 'Now, the region.' Two names surface from beyond the ridge: Tomita. Akagi." — keeps register uniform, loses the callback; (B) Keep as-is, treating "the room is clean" as an intentional player-facing leitmotif — accepts the modern/meta tonal note at the single most-read tier-climax beat; (C) Keep the phrase but quote/frame it so it reads as deliberate echo rather than literal narration. Which do you want, and is the "clean your room" phrasing meant to ever reach the player?

confirmedFindings.title: Mechanics / UI-instruction parentheticals leak into diegetic event-log lines, breaking register
confirmedFindings.evidence: V1 ends its in-fiction line with a UI affordance instruction: *"…'Folk say a kappa takes children at the ford…' (You may look, or not.)"*. V3 ends with a balance-cap reminder: *"…The first bolt is graded and sold under the house's name. (Trade is one strand of one pillar — no more.)"* — the latter literally restates the ≤⅓ Estate & Wealth hard-cap that is already given in the same row's design cell ('(Trade hard-capped ≤⅓ of Estate & Wealth — §2.16.)') and enforced in §2.16.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: These are the design/systems voice (player-instruction + a balance invariant) wearing the costume of in-fiction event-log prose. Most §3 log lines stay inside the fiction; these two break POV/voice and read as AI-doc filler. The V3 parenthetical is pure mechanics ('one strand of one pillar') — jarringly un-diegetic in a line otherwise spoken by/about Onatsu and the silk.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Strip the mechanics/UI parentheticals from the diegetic copy (the cap is enforced in §2.16 and stated in the design cell; optionality is already a system property). If player-facing optionality cues are wanted, render them as UI chrome, not as in-fiction event-log text.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Quotes verified verbatim: docs/prd.md:1907 (V1) ends its diegetic event-log line with "(You may look, or not.)" and :1909 (V3) ends with "(Trade is one strand of one pillar — no more.)". The column is explicitly labelled "Diegetic event-log line(s)", and of the eight V-rung lines (V0–V7) only these two append a mechanics/UI parenthetical to in-fiction prose, so they are genuine register outliers. Both also duplicate their own design cell: V3's parenthetical restates the cap already given as "(Trade hard-capped ≤⅓ of Estate & Wealth — §2.16.)", and V1's optionality cue restates the design cell's "the 'kappa' of the ford; look, or not". The finding is accurate. Severity downgraded from claimed medium to low — this is prose-tone polish on illustrative example copy in a spec, not a functional/design defect. Fix kept as needs-human because whether to keep the cues as a deliberate fourth-wall nudge vs. strip them is a taste/voice call the human arbitrates, not a clerical edit.
confirmedFindings.question: Two T1 diegetic event-log lines (docs/prd.md:1907 V1, :1909 V3) close with non-diegetic parentheticals that the other six V-rung lines avoid and that duplicate their own design cell: V1's "(You may look, or not.)" repeats the design cell's "look, or not", and V3's "(Trade is one strand of one pillar — no more.)" restates the "(Trade hard-capped ≤⅓ of Estate & Wealth — §2.16.)" cap. Options: (A) strip both parentheticals so the event-log column stays purely in-fiction — cleanest register, no info lost (optionality and cap both already live in the design cell + §2.16/§2.13); (B) keep them as a deliberate player-facing fourth-wall nudge inside the narration; (C) move such cues to UI-chrome callouts rather than diegetic log text. Which do you want — and is the parenthetical aside an intended house style for the event log, or an inconsistency to normalize?

confirmedFindings.title: G6 (spine) and O5 (Origin) climactic lines are near-duplicates that fire at the same moment
confirmedFindings.evidence: G6: *"…And down-valley: she is real, and grown, and not you. 'Tama ran. Tama lived.' You have a name again: Tahei. You set it down quietly, and pick the house's work back up."* O5: *"She is real, and grown, and not you. 'Tama ran. Tama lived.' And you have a name again: Tahei. You set it down quietly — and pick your work back up."* — they share the same four sentences nearly verbatim. O5 is defined as coinciding with G6 ('coincides with & DEEPENS the spine G6 beat', line 2097), so a player who completes the Origin track sees both at the same beat.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The design intent is that O5 *deepens* G6, but the wording repeats it almost word-for-word, so for an Origin-completing player the climax reads as copy-paste / a doubled log line rather than a richer beat — and for a player who skips Origin the spine line already carries the full Tahei-naming payload the §5/§3.6.2 spec says lives on the Origin track. It undercuts the emotional peak of the whole game.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Differentiate the two lines so O5 adds something G6 doesn't (the present-day family witnessing the name, the reunions completing) rather than restating 'Tama ran / a name again / set it down quietly'. Decide which line owns the 'Tahei' naming so it isn't spoken twice.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at docs/prd.md:2027 (G6 spine line) and :2097 (O5 Origin capstone). The two share four near-verbatim sentences ("she is real, and grown, and not you" / "'Tama ran. Tama lived.'" / "you have a name again: Tahei" / "set it down quietly … pick [the house's/your] work back up"), and O5's own header (line 2097) defines it as "coincides with & DEEPENS the spine G6 beat" — so an Origin-completing player gets both at the same moment, reading as copy-paste rather than a deepened beat. Beyond duplication there is a genuine internal inconsistency: §3.6.2 (lines 2088, 2096-2097) and the G6 cell body (line 2027) repeatedly state Tahei claiming his true name is the OPTIONAL Origin O5 capstone payoff ("a late, de-emphasised side beat"), yet the guaranteed G6 spine log line already contains "You have a name again: Tahei" — leaking the Origin-gated naming to players who skip Origin. No log-line variant is noted that would resolve the overlap, so it cannot be refuted. New (not a mechanics restatement); requires authorial/design judgment to rewrite and assign ownership, hence needs-human. Medium because it sits at the emotional climax and embeds a real payoff-ownership contradiction, though it is a doc-level wording issue rather than a mechanical defect.
confirmedFindings.question: The G6 spine event-log line (docs/prd.md:2027) and the optional Origin O5 capstone line (docs/prd.md:2097) share four near-verbatim sentences and fire at the same beat, so for an Origin-completing player the climax reads as a doubled/copy-paste log entry; worse, the G6 spine line already includes "You have a name again: Tahei," which §3.6.2 (lines 2088, 2096-2097) says is the OPTIONAL Origin-track payoff, so a non-Origin player also receives the naming the spec reserves for O5. Decision needed: (a) Which rung owns the word "Tahei"? Option 1 — G6 spine keeps ONLY the Otsuru truth-reveal ("she is real, and grown, and not you / Tama ran / lived") and drops the Tahei naming, so naming lands solely at O5 (cleanly honoring "naming is Origin-gated," but a player who skips Origin never hears him reclaim his name — possibly the intended cost of skipping). Option 2 — keep the naming on the guaranteed spine and instead make O5 add something G6 cannot (the present-day family witnessing the name, the reunions completing among his own people), turning O5 into a genuine deepening rather than an echo. (b) Either way, O5's line should be rewritten so it complements rather than restates G6. Trade-off: Option 1 maximizes design consistency with §3.6.2 but risks leaving the spine-only ending emotionally thinner; Option 2 preserves a full payoff for every player but formally contradicts the "naming lives on Origin" rule unless that rule is relaxed. Which do you want?

confirmedFindings.title: G0 dream-thread aside is a muddled/over-reaching image
confirmedFindings.evidence: G0 closes: *"…'I'll run yours, for old times' sake.' One route. One load. (Why does his face ache to remember?)"* — 'his face ache to remember' attributes the aching/remembering to Kanta's face rather than to the MC who half-recognizes it.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The Origin thread's dream-asides are supposed to be the lightest touch (returning memory, ZERO bonus). This one is grammatically tangled — a face neither aches nor remembers — so the image reads as slightly purple/AI-ish at the very first Origin contact, where clarity matters most.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Re-aim the ache at the MC and the half-recognition (candidate: '(Why does that face tug at something you can't name?)').
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: see above
confirmedFindings.question: The G0 dream-aside (docs/prd.md:2021) ends "(Why does his face ache to remember?)", which grammatically pins the ache-to-remember on Kanta's face — yet narratively the half-recognition belongs to the MC (the amnesiac Tahei), and Kanta is the one who already remembers. Reword so the ache/recognition sits with the MC? Options: (a) re-aim at the MC, e.g. "(Why does that face tug at something you can't name?)"; (b) keep "his face" but make it the trigger, e.g. "(Why does his face pull at a memory you can't reach?)"; (c) leave as intentional dreamy ambiguity. Trade-off: (a)/(b) sharpen the Origin-thread theme and remove the misattribution at first contact; (c) preserves the existing fuzzy-poetic register but keeps the slightly tangled image.

confirmedFindings.title: Kanbei's in-household rival thread is introduced in v1 but never resolved or handed off
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §1.8 cast (line 511) & §5 T2.4 (line 3545); absent from §5 T3.4/T3.5 roster (lines 3704-3721) and §5 T4.4 (lines 3800-3811)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.8 line 511: 'Kanbei (later) | Jealous middling retainer ... In-household antagonist-rival who sees the rising stray as a threat; player-influenceable détente or self-inflicted washout. | T2+'. §5 T2.4 line 3545 confirms he 'joins this tier' (T2/v1): 'Kanbei (jealous middling retainer — an in-household antagonist-rival, player-influenceable détente or self-inflicted washout, *not evil*).' Grep shows Kanbei appears ONLY at lines 511 and 3545 — he is not in any G0-G7 rung, not in §5 T2.5 NPC beats, not in §5 T2.7 side-quests, and not in the T3 stub roster (§5 T3.4/T3.5) or T4 roadmap roster (§5 T4.4). There is also no hosting mechanic: §2.15 reputation covers per-shop/per-family/headman, not in-household retainers, so the promised 'player-influenceable détente' has no system.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The v1-boundary requirement is that every T0-T2 thread resolves or deliberately hands off to the stub. Kanbei is an in-household antagonist-rival explicitly seeded in v1 (T2) with a promised player-influenced outcome (détente or washout), yet that outcome is never beated, has no host system, and is not carried into the T3 stub or T4 roadmap. He is a thread that simply stops — a small but real loose end a completionist v1 player will notice.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either (a) author a v1 resolution beat / hosting mechanic for Kanbei's détente-or-washout (e.g. a T2 side-quest in §5 T2.7), or (b) explicitly defer his arc to T3+ in the parked list and the T3 roster, or (c) downgrade him to a static flavour card with no promised 'player-influenceable' outcome.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed by grep + read: "Kanbei" occurs ONLY at line 511 (§1.8 cast) and line 3545 (§5 T2.4 roster). Both frame him as an in-household antagonist-rival introduced in v1/T2 with a promised "player-influenceable détente or self-inflicted washout." He is absent from T2.5 NPC beats (3563-3593, which lists Origin cast + region rivals Hanzaki/Tomita/Akagi but not Kanbei), from T2.7 side-quests (3624-3646), and from the T3.4/T3.5 and T4.4 forward rosters. No in-household-retainer hosting mechanic exists (§2.15 reputation is per-shop/family/headman). So a v1-introduced interactive thread has no resolution beat and no supporting system — a real loose end. Downgraded from claimed medium to low: Kanbei is a single non-spine flavour roster NPC who gates nothing, and the two retainers introduced in the same bullet (Saburozaemon, Heisuke) are likewise unbeated, so the line reads as intentionally high-level roster flavour; the only genuine defect is the unmet "player-influenceable" promise. Not clerical — resolving it (author a beat vs defer vs downgrade wording) is a design judgement, hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: Kanbei is seeded in v1 (§1.8 line 511, §5 T2.4 line 3545) as an in-household antagonist-rival with an explicitly "player-influenceable détente or self-inflicted washout," but there is no resolution beat (absent from T2.5/T2.7) and no host system (§2.15 reputation does not model in-household retainers). Which do you want: (a) author a lightweight T2.7 side-quest / NPC beat plus a minimal in-household-standing hook so the détente-or-washout is actually playable (most work, delivers the promise); (b) keep him in v1 but reword §1.8/§T2.4 to drop "player-influenceable," making him a static washout/flavour card (cheap, removes the unmet promise); or (c) defer his interactive arc to T3+ by adding him to the §T3.7 parked list and a forward roster line (cheap, preserves the promise but pushes it past v1)? Note Saburozaemon and Heisuke are introduced in the same bullet with no beats either, so the underlying question is whether T2.4 roster lines are meant to imply authored arcs at all.

confirmedFindings.title: Name's per-deed cap (§4.2.1) uses a band-top of 10K/80K, but its seasonal normalizer TIER_REF_NAME (§4.2.2) uses 15K/140K — two conflicting 'band-tops' for the same pillar/tier, and the cap formula's stated basis is undefined for ungated Name
confirmedFindings.evidence: §4.2.1: the per-deed cap is computed against "that pillar's own band-top for the current tier (= its §4.1 gate threshold)" (L2381). But §4.1's gate table names Name only at T3 (≥500K) / T4 (≥6M) — Name is UNGATED in v1 (T0–T2), so 'its §4.1 gate threshold' does not exist. The Name rows give caps T0 40 · T1 400 · T2 3.2K (L2401-2402) = 0.04 × {1K, 10K, 80K} (the §4.0 display-band tops). Meanwhile §4.2.2 sets Name's reference TIER_REF_NAME = armsGate+estateGate+officeGate = "15K (T1) / 140K (T2)" (L2479, L2502). So Name's 'band-top' is 10K for the cap but 15K for the seasonal at T1 (80K vs 140K at T2).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The whole §4.2 model insists each pillar has one band-top per tier (the basis for both the per-event cap and the seasonal normalizer). For Name the two streams silently use different magnitudes, and the cap formula references a §4.1 gate that does not exist for Name — so any future retune of Name's reference must update two numbers that the spec claims are one concept, the canonical drift trap this lens hunts. (Functionally the current Name deed bases 160/180/1.6K/1.8K still sit under either cap, so it is not yet a balance break — but it is an unflagged definitional contradiction.)
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one Name band-top per tier and use it for both the per-event cap and TIER_REF_NAME, and replace the cap formula's '= its §4.1 gate threshold' wording with an explicit ungated-pillar rule (e.g. 'for ungated pillars the cap basis is TIER_REF_NAME').
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified against docs/prd.md. §4.2.1 (L2380-2382) states the per-deed cap is computed against "that pillar's own band-top for the current tier (= its §4.1 gate threshold)"; the Name cap row (L2401-2402) gives T0 40 / T1 400 / T2 3.2K = 0.04 × {1K, 10K, 80K}, i.e. the §4.0 DISPLAY-band tops (L2284-2286). But §4.2.2 (L2479, L2502) sets Name's seasonal normalizer TIER_REF_NAME = armsGate+estateGate+officeGate = 15K (T1) / 140K (T2). And §4.1 (L2319-2320) gates Name only at T3/T4, so Name has NO §4.1 gate in v1 — the cap formula's parenthetical "(= its §4.1 gate threshold)" has no referent for Name. So Name carries two different "band-top" magnitudes (10K/80K for the per-deed cap vs 15K/140K for the seasonal normalizer), and the cap basis is described via a gate that does not exist. The quotes and arithmetic are accurate and this is distinct from the F2/F4 normalization fixes already in the doc. Downgraded severity from claimed medium to low because the finding itself concedes there is no functional/balance break (Name bases 160/180/1.6K/1.8K sit under either cap) — it is a documentation/definitional drift-trap, not an active inconsistency in behavior. Note also a partial mitigant: the two normalizers operate in different unit spaces (the cap bounds Name's displayed VALUE; the seasonal normalizer bounds Name's BASIS = sum of three other pillars' high-waters), so they are arguably not strictly "the same concept" — which is why this needs a human design call rather than a mechanical reconciliation.
confirmedFindings.question: Name has no §4.1 gate in v1 (gated only at T3/T4), yet §4.2.1's per-deed cap formula bases the cap on "(= its §4.1 gate threshold)" and the Name cap numbers (40/400/3.2K) actually use the §4.0 display band-tops (1K/10K/80K), while §4.2.2's seasonal normalizer TIER_REF_NAME uses a different magnitude (15K/140K = sum of the three gates). Which magnitude should be canonical for Name's per-deed cap basis? Option A: keep the §4.0 display band (1K/10K/80K → caps 40/400/3.2K) and reword the formula to add an explicit ungated-pillar rule (e.g. "for ungated pillars the cap basis is the §4.0 display band-top, not a gate"). Option B: align it to TIER_REF_NAME (15K/140K → caps 600/5.6K) so both streams share one number. Trade-off: Option A preserves current cap headroom and matches Name's own displayed value band but leaves two distinct Name magnitudes that a future retune must keep in sync; Option B unifies the two numbers but raises Name's per-deed cap and conflates Name's displayed-value space with its sum-of-three-pillars basis space. Either way the "(= its §4.1 gate threshold)" gloss must be corrected since Name has no v1 gate.

confirmedFindings.title: The per-deed cap (= PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION · band-top) is a pure derivation hand-typed in four places — the structural twin of the JUDGE_K drift round 2 already caught
confirmedFindings.evidence: The cap is defined once as a formula — `jump = min(deedBaseValue, PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION * tierPillarBandTop(...))`, `PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION = 0.04` (L2374, L2377) — then the resulting numbers are written out by hand at least four times: §4.2.1 prose "T0 Estate cap = 0.04·0.8K = 32 ip, T0 Arms = 0.04·0.5K = 20 ip, T1 Office = 0.04·2K = 80 ip, T2 Arms = 0.04·30K = 1.2K, T2 Office = 0.04·50K = 2K" (L2381-2382); the §4.2.1 table's rightmost column (L2390-2402); §4.8.2 "Estate 8K → ≤320; Arms 5K → ≤200; Office 2K → ≤80" (L2998); §4.8.3 "Estate 60K → ≤2.4K; Arms 30K → ≤1.2K; Office 50K → ≤2K" (L3022). All four are 0.04 × the §4.1 gate; the values happen to agree now, but it is the identical write-it-out-by-hand pattern that drifted in JUDGE_K (R1 numbers-balance-2).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Any change to PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION or a gate threshold requires hand-editing four scattered locations in lockstep; miss one and the deed bases (which are tuned to sit at/under cap, several labelled '(cap)') silently violate the cap discipline. This is the same single-source-of-truth hazard the lens targets, on a value that is 100% derivable.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: State the per-deed cap once as the formula and stop tabulating the products; or mark the cap columns/restatements 'derived = 0.04·gate, generated' and let docs/balance/ emit them. Have the verifier assert every deed base ≤ 0.04·band-top so an at-cap base can't drift over.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified verbatim: the per-deed cap (= 0.04·§4.1 band-top) is hand-tabulated in four places — §4.2.1 prose (L2381-2382), the §4.2.1 table's rightmost 'per-deed cap (0.04·band-top)' column (L2390-2402), §4.8.2 (L2998: Estate 8K→≤320; Arms 5K→≤200; Office 2K→≤80), and §4.8.3 (L3022: Estate 60K→≤2.4K; Arms 30K→≤1.2K; Office 50K→≤2K). The formula+constant are stated once at L2374/L2377 (PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION=0.04), so all four numeric restatements are pure derivations. This is a real single-source-of-truth duplication and is distinct from the round-2 JUDGE_K item (different constant/locations), so it is new and unfixed. Downgraded to LOW because there is no current drift — I checked every product and all agree, so it is a latent maintainability hazard rather than a live internal-inconsistency. Fix requires design judgment (strip numbers, flag as derived, or add verifier assertion), hence needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: The per-deed cap numbers (0.04·band-top) are restated by hand in four spots (§4.2.1 prose + table column, §4.8.2, §4.8.3) while the formula and PER_EVENT_CAP_FRACTION=0.04 are already stated once — they agree today but are not independent levers. Per the 'generate, don't duplicate' convention, should we (a) keep the formula + a single generated cap table under docs/content/ and remove the inline cap restatements from §4.8.2/§4.8.3 and the §4.2.1 prose; (b) keep the expository numbers but explicitly tag each 'derived = 0.04·§4.1 gate (not an independent lever)'; or (c) leave as-is and instead add a §6.6 verifier assertion that every deed base ≤ 0.04·band-top so an at-cap base can't silently drift over? Trade-off: (a) is cleanest for single-source but loses at-a-glance reading; (b) is cheap and low-risk; (c) catches drift at runtime but leaves the doc duplication.

confirmedFindings.title: §6.6/§7 emit balance tables to docs/balance/, but the CLAUDE.md convention says generated content/balance tables land under docs/content/
confirmedFindings.evidence: CLAUDE.md (project instructions, Layout/Conventions): "Generated content/balance tables will land under `docs/content/`." But §6.6 writes "`docs/balance/curves.md`" (L4133) and the milestones repeatedly reference the `docs/balance/` directory: M4 "the regenerated ranks/areas/influence/balance docs" (L4620), M6 "the generated docs (`docs/balance/`, `docs/content/`) are current" (L4690), M7 "regenerates docs/balance/ + docs/content/" (L4698).
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The convention doc and the build plan disagree on where generated balance tables live. A reader following CLAUDE.md will look in docs/content/ and find balance curves under docs/balance/ instead; gen:docs --check wiring and any tooling that asserts the output path could target the wrong directory.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Pick one location for generated balance tables and make CLAUDE.md and §6.6/§7 agree (either add docs/balance/ to the CLAUDE.md Layout note, or move curves.md under docs/content/).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: docs/prd.md §6.6 (L4133) writes generated balance tables to `docs/balance/curves.md`, and §7.2 milestones reference the `docs/balance/` directory for regenerated balance docs (M4 L4620, M6 L4690, M7 L4698) — consistently treating `docs/balance/` (curves) and `docs/content/` (bestiary/areas/ranks/influence) as two distinct output directories. CLAUDE.md Layout (L69-70) says "Generated content/balance tables will land under `docs/content/`", which conflicts on where balance tables live. Genuine cross-doc contradiction, not a rounds-1-2 restatement, not already fixed. Low severity (documentation/path inconsistency that could mislead a reader or a gen:docs path assertion). Marked needs-human, not trivial-apply, because: (1) the error is in CLAUDE.md, which is outside the reviewed file (docs/prd.md is internally consistent), so there is no clean verbatim PRD edit; and (2) choosing the canonical location requires a design decision.
confirmedFindings.question: Generated balance tables: the PRD consistently emits them to `docs/balance/` (e.g. `docs/balance/curves.md`, plus M4/M6/M7 references), keeping `docs/content/` for content tables (bestiary/areas/ranks/influence) — a deliberate two-directory split. CLAUDE.md's Layout note instead says both "content/balance tables will land under `docs/content/`". Which is canonical? Option A (recommended, lower-churn): keep the PRD's two-directory split and update the CLAUDE.md line to read "Generated balance tables land under `docs/balance/` and content tables under `docs/content/`" — the PRD already references docs/balance/ in 4+ places. Option B: collapse to a single `docs/content/` directory and rewrite the PRD's `docs/balance/curves.md` plus the M4/M6/M7 `docs/balance/` references. Either way, gen:docs/--check output paths must match the chosen convention.

confirmedFindings.title: The four-pillar table (kanji + domain + grows-on) is hand-duplicated in full in §1.6.1 and §2.16 (plus partial restatements in §1.13/§1.14/§2.22/§5) — a duplication surface with no canonical source
confirmedFindings.evidence: §1.6.1 (L374-379) and §2.16 (L1432-1437) carry near-identical pillar tables — Arms 武威 / Estate & Wealth 家産 / Standing & Office 官威 / Name & Honour 家格, each with protagonist domain and 'grows on' text. Round 1 already caught a stale variant of exactly this class (ADR D-008 still listing rejected 政威, R1 cross-references-6).
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Declare §1.6.1 the canonical pillar table (it carries the resolution note), have §2.16 point to it rather than re-copy, and treat the pillar registry (id/kanji/romaji/domain) as a generated table (docs/content/influence.md) so §2.16 and the prose restatements render from one source.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Confirmed at source: §1.6.1 (docs/prd.md L374-379) and §2.16 (L1432-1437) carry two near-identical four-pillar tables — Arms 武威 / Estate & Wealth 家産 / Standing & Office 官威 / Name & Honour 家格, each with protagonist domain + 'grows on' text. The duplication is genuine and squarely the generate-dont-duplicate lens's target: pillar identity (kanji/romaji/domain) is hand-maintained in two full tables plus prose restatements. The finding is honest that there is no LIVE drift today (官威 consistent everywhere; 政威 only in rejected-notes), so this is a preventive/structural gap, not an active inconsistency — hence low. It is genuinely NEW vs R1 cross-references-6, which flagged a STALE divergent value (D-008 listing rejected 政威); this flags the duplicate-table SURFACE itself. Not clerical: deciding whether a vision-overview table should be collapsed to a cross-reference and whether the pillar registry should be promoted to generated content is an editorial/design judgment, so needs-human.
confirmedFindings.question: The four-pillar identity (name/kanji/romaji/domain) is hand-duplicated in two full tables (§1.6.1 vision overview L374-379 and §2.16 systems L1432-1437) plus prose restatements. Today they are consistent, but any kanji/domain edit must be propagated by hand. How do you want to de-duplicate? Options: (A) Leave as-is — the two tables intentionally serve different altitudes (vision vs systems) and the redundancy aids readability; accept the maintenance risk given low edit frequency. (B) Declare §1.6.1 canonical (it carries the 官威-vs-政威 resolution note) and collapse §2.16's table to a cross-reference, keeping only the accrual/gating detail §2.16 adds. (C) Promote the pillar registry (id/kanji/romaji/domain) to a generated table under docs/content/ (per the 'generate, don't duplicate' convention) so both sections and the prose restatements render from one source. Trade-off: (A) is zero-effort but keeps a drift surface; (B) is cheap and removes one table but still leaves prose restatements hand-maintained; (C) fully removes the drift surface but adds tooling now for content that doesn't yet exist as game data.

confirmedFindings.title: Growth curves use `^` (power), which ECMAScript does NOT guarantee correctly-rounded — cross-machine/cross-build byte-identical replay is unprovable, and the §6.1 determinism lint has a matching hole
confirmedFindings.location: docs/prd.md §6.1 lint (lines 3903-3907); §4.5.1 line 2688; §4.7 lines 1008, 2887, 2873-2874; promise sites §6.1 line 3901, §6.3 lines 4025-4027, §6.7 lines 4155-4156, §6.12 line 4282; qa-playtesting.md line 23-24
confirmedFindings.evidence: The headline determinism promise is cross-machine/cross-build: §6.1 line 3901 'a fixed seed + a fixed intent/tick script produces a byte-identical GameState'; §6.12 line 4282 'Replays are byte-identical (Vitest-asserted)'; qa-playtesting.md line 23 'a fixed (seed, intent-script) reproduces byte-identically — every bug is reproducible'. The §6.1 determinism lint bans only nondeterministic *inputs*: line 3905-3906 '`no DOM/canvas/window/`document`/`localStorage`/`indexedDB`/`Date.now`/`performance.now` in `src/core/`'' and 3903 'no `Math.random()`'. It says NOTHING about `Math.pow`/`Math.exp`/`Math.log`/trig. But the core's growth curves are powers: §4.5.1 line 2688 'xpForLevel(L) = round( XP_BASE * XP_GROWTH^(L-1) )'; §4.7 'cost = base · r^owned' (1008), 'cost(n) = producerBase · PRODUCER_GROWTH^owned' (2887), '1.25^tier' (2873). ECMAScript only mandates +,−,×,÷ and `Math.sqrt` correctly-rounded (IEEE-754); `Math.pow`/`exp`/`log`/trig are implementation-approximated and differ across engines and even V8 versions. A naive `Math.pow(XP_GROWTH, L-1)` can differ by 1 ULP across machines, and `round(...)` near an x.5 boundary then flips an integer XP/cost threshold → the same seed reaches a rung at a different tick on a different machine, desyncing every replay (and a saved fight that re-derives any powered stat). Note the seasonal engine (§4.2.2) is safe precisely because it uses only ÷, clamp, and `sqrt` — no `pow`.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the one cross-machine determinism break in the whole core. The CI pacing gate (§4.8.4: 'a headless playthrough that clears any grind rung in < ~28 min ... fails the pacing test') and 'a saved fight resume[s] identically' (§6.7) both rest on byte-identical replay being reproducible on any build/runner. The lint that exists *specifically to enforce determinism* lets the one function family that can break it through.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Every `^` in the spec has an INTEGER exponent (level, owned, tier), so all can be computed by repeated multiplication (× is correctly-rounded → fully deterministic). Mandate an integer-power helper for all `r^n` curves and EXTEND the §6.1 core lint to ban `Math.pow`/`Math.exp`/`Math.log`/`Math.sin`/`Math.cos`/`Math.tan` (whitelist `Math.sqrt`, which is correctly-rounded and used by §4.2.2). Alternatively, scope the determinism promise to 'same engine build' — but that weakens the QA substrate.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason:
Confirmed verbatim: the growth curves all use `^` with INTEGER exponents — §4.5.1 line 2688 `round(XP_BASE * XP_GROWTH^(L-1))`, §4.7 lines 2831/2887 `r^owned`/`PRODUCER_GROWTH^owned`, §4.7.2 line 2873 `1.25^tier` — while the §6.1 determinism lint (3903-3907) bans only `Math.random()` and DOM/`Date.now`/`performance.now`, saying nothing about `Math.pow`/`exp`/`log`/trig. The determinism promise is stated absolutely and cross-machine (§6.1 line 3901, §6.7 line 4156, §6.12 line 4282 'byte-identical', qa-playtesting.md line 24 'every bug is reproducible'). The technical core is correct: ECMAScript/IEEE-754 guarantees correctly-rounded results only for +,−,×,÷ and `Math.sqrt`; `Math.pow` is implementation-approximated and can differ by ULPs across engines/builds, and `round()` near an x.5 boundary can then flip an integer XP/cost level threshold. Because §6.8 recomputes all derived stats (levels from xp) on load, an exported save reloaded under a different engine could re-derive a different level and desync the replay. Grep confirms the fix family (integer-power-by-multiplication / ban Math.pow) is nowhere in the PRD, and the seasonal engine's sqrt-only safety is noted but not generalized — so this is genuinely NEW and unaddressed. Severity tempered to medium (not the claimed high): the CI determinism test runs on a single engine and is unaffected, so the practical break is limited to cross-engine save portability and to the spec overpromising an absolute cross-machine guarantee; the rounding-boundary ULP collision is rare. Still a real spec-completeness gap in the lint that exists specifically to enforce a LOCKED determinism principle. Marked needs-human because the resolution is a policy choice (strengthen the guarantee vs. re-scope the wording), not a clerical edit.</verdict_reason>
<parameter name="refined_question">Determinism policy for growth curves: the determinism promise (§6.1/§6.7/§6.12 'byte-identical replay', qa-playtesting.md 'every bug is reproducible') is worded as cross-machine/cross-build, but the §6.1 core lint bans only nondeterministic inputs (Math.random, Date.now, DOM) — it does not ban `Math.pow`/`Math.exp`/`Math.log`/trig, and every growth curve (xpForLevel `XP_GROWTH^(L-1)`, producer `r^owned`, quality `1.25^tier`) uses `^`. ECMAScript only mandates correctly-rounded +,−,×,÷ and `Math.sqrt`; `Math.pow` can differ by 1 ULP across engines/builds, and a `round()` near an x.5 boundary can then flip an integer level/cost threshold, so the same seed/save can re-derive a different level on a different browser (derived stats are recomputed on load per §6.8). Do we (a) mandate an integer-power-by-repeated-multiplication helper for all `r^n` curves (every exponent is integer, so × is correctly-rounded and fully deterministic) AND extend the §6.1 core lint to ban Math.pow/exp/log/sin/cos/tan (whitelisting Math.sqrt, used by §4.2.2) — making byte-identical replay hold across machines/builds at the cost of a small core constraint and helper; or (b) accept that byte-identical replay is guaranteed only within one JS-engine build and re-word the §6.1/§6.7/§6.12 promises and the qa-playtesting determinism claim accordingly (weakening the QA reproducibility substrate but requiring no code constraint)? Trade-off: (a) is cheap to implement and preserves the strong guarantee; (b) avoids any core constraint but downgrades a LOCKED-canon determinism principle and the 'every bug reproducible from a save' workflow.</refined_question>
</invoke>
confirmedFindings.question: Determinism policy: do we (a) mandate integer-power-by-repeated-multiplication for all growth curves and extend the §6.1 lint to ban non-correctly-rounded Math functions (Math.pow/exp/log/trig) in core — making 'byte-identical replay' hold across machines/builds; or (b) accept that byte-identical replay is only guaranteed within one JS-engine build and re-word the §6.1/§6.7/§6.12 promises and the qa-playtesting determinism claim accordingly?

confirmedFindings.title: The chosen save stack (IndexedDB, single autosave) is unreliable in itch.io's cross-origin game iframe — the deploy target no lens audited
confirmedFindings.evidence: §6.8 picks IndexedDB "chosen over `localStorage` for robustness and capacity" and §7.3 ships "`vite build` emits a static `dist/`… zipped and uploaded to **itch.io**". But itch.io serves HTML games inside a SANDBOXED CROSS-ORIGIN iframe (html-classic.itch.zone) embedded on the itch.io page. Modern browser storage partitioning makes third-party-iframe IndexedDB/localStorage either partitioned or blocked: Safari/ITP blocks third-party storage by default; Firefox dFPI partitions per top-level site; Chrome storage-partitioning is rolling out. Worse, itch's "run fullscreen"/"launch in new tab" button changes the game from a 3rd-party iframe to a 1st-party top-level context — a DIFFERENT storage partition — so the same player can silently get two saves or lose one by toggling fullscreen. Nowhere in the PRD is iframe/embedding/partitioned-storage mentioned (grep: zero hits for iframe/embed/partition/third-party). The M7 smoke test (4711, 4724, 4726) only checks "autosave persists across reload" on the draft URL in one browser/context — it does not test the embedded-vs-fullscreen partition mismatch or Safari. The base64 export (§7.3) is the only backup but is opt-in; a player learns to export only AFTER losing the 28.5h single autosave. This is the canon's own 'robust/durable' claim being inverted by the locked deploy target.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: This is the literal definition of a 28.5h save being silently lost on the actual hosting platform — the single highest-impact correctness gap, and it lives in a category (deploy-target/browser-storage reality) that no round-1/2 lens owned.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Decide a storage-survival strategy for the itch iframe BEFORE M6/M7: (a) require launch in own tab/fullscreen (first-party context) and detect/refuse the embedded iframe; and/or (b) on first load, probe IndexedDB writability and, on failure or a fresh store that 'should' have a save, surface a prominent recovery/import prompt; and (c) make base64 export a first-class, frequently-nudged backup rather than an opt-in safety net. Add an explicit itch-iframe storage smoke test (Safari + Firefox + embedded-vs-fullscreen) to M7's done criteria.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified verbatim: §6.8 (prd.md:4164-4165) picks IndexedDB single-autosave "over localStorage for robustness," §7.3 (4751-4752) ships the static dist/ to itch.io, and M7 (4711-4712, 4724, 4726) specifies only a single unscoped "fresh-browser smoke test" with no browser matrix and no embedded-vs-fullscreen distinction. Grep confirms ZERO hits for iframe/embed/partition/third-party/fullscreen/cross-origin anywhere in the PRD, so the deploy target's iframe storage reality is genuinely unaddressed — a NEW gap in a category (host/browser storage reality) no prior lens owned. The core concern is legitimate: itch.io serves HTML games in a cross-origin iframe, third-party-iframe IndexedDB is partitioned (Chrome/Firefox) or blocked-by-default (Safari/WebKit ITP), and the single autosave's only backup (base64 export, 4759-4761) is explicitly opt-in. HOWEVER the finding's catastrophic framing is partly refuted: (1) the "toggle fullscreen → new partition → two saves / lost save" mechanism is wrong — itch's fullscreen uses the Fullscreen API on the same iframe, the top-level site stays itch.io, so the partition does not change, and itch provides no first-party "new tab" launch for embedded HTML games; (2) partitioned storage persists fine on Chrome/Firefox in normal use (the whole idle-game genre ships on itch this way), so "28.5h silently lost" is a Safari/private-mode edge, not the default outcome. Real and worth a human decision, but not clearly critical — downgraded to high. Needs-human because the resolution is a design choice (browser matrix + storage-survival strategy), not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: itch.io serves HTML games inside a cross-origin iframe (html-classic.itch.zone), where third-party-iframe IndexedDB is partitioned per top-level site on Chrome/Firefox and blocked-by-default on Safari/WebKit. The PRD's "robust, durable" single IndexedDB autosave (§6.8) and opt-in-only base64 backup (§7.3) are never reality-checked against this host, and M7's "fresh-browser smoke test" (§7.2 M7, §7.3) names no browser matrix. What storage-survival posture should v1 commit to, and what must M7's done criteria require? Options/trade-offs: (a) Accept partitioned storage as-is (works on Chrome/Firefox, the dominant itch audience) but ADD an explicit M7 done-criterion that the autosave-persists-across-reload smoke test runs in the embedded itch iframe on Chromium AND WebKit/Safari (cheap; catches the real Safari-block case before publish). (b) On first load, probe IndexedDB writability/persistence and, on failure, surface a prominent recovery/import banner instead of silently running with a volatile store (moderate dev cost; turns silent loss into a visible warning). (c) Promote base64 export from opt-in safety net to a first-class, periodically-nudged backup (e.g. milestone-based export prompts) so a single store wipe is never unrecoverable (low cost, high payoff for a 28.5h single-slot game). (d) Detect the embedded iframe and nudge "open fullscreen" — note this does NOT change the storage partition, so it is cosmetic for storage and should be dropped from the rationale. Recommended: (c) + the WebKit/Safari line in (a) as M7 done-criteria, with (b) as a stretch. Which combination, and which browsers must M7 cover?

confirmedFindings.title: Emoji are a load-bearing art element but render completely differently per OS/browser — the locked register fights the locked anti-slop aesthetic
confirmedFindings.evidence: D-013 locks "Art register = TEXT + EMOJI + CSS" and §2.21/§6.9 make emoji a carrier of pillar identity, season tags, rarity, and log bullets. ui-design.md §7 locks a set (🏯 ⛩️ 🎐 🍁 🍶 🏮 …) and tames them with one CSS filter: ".emoji { filter: sepia(.25) saturate(.7) brightness(.95) contrast(1.05); }". But a single emoji codepoint renders as entirely different artwork on Apple vs Google vs Microsoft vs Samsung vs Linux — and a uniform CSS filter applied on top of those divergent base glyphs cannot produce a 'consistent desaturated woodblock' look; on platforms with no colour-emoji font (many Linux/embedded browsers) the same glyphs render as monochrome outlines or tofu boxes, and variation-selector glyphs like ⛩️/🎐 frequently fail to render at all. The ui-design risk row only guards "emoji spam / inconsistent register → one curated set" — it addresses quantity, never cross-platform glyph variance. §7.4.1 R4 even calls the register "low-risk… no asset pipeline", which is exactly why the look is uncontrolled. This collides directly with the first-class pillar 'must look intentional — woodblock/ink, NOT generic AI-slop'.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: The aesthetic bar is a stated first-class pillar; shipping platform-dependent or tofu'd emoji is precisely the 'AI-slop' failure the project explicitly forbids, and no lens has audited cross-platform glyph fidelity.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Either bundle a controlled emoji font (e.g. Twemoji/Noto-Emoji subset) so every platform renders the same base glyphs, or replace the load-bearing period motifs with the inline-SVG path ui-design already permits (line 4) for pillar/season/rarity marks, leaving Unicode emoji only where variance is harmless. Add a cross-platform emoji-rendering check to the capture-game-states sweep.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified: D-013 (prd.md:778) and §2.21 (1654) / §6.9 (4210) lock "Art register = TEXT + EMOJI + CSS" with emoji carrying pillar identity, season tags, rarity, and log bullets; ui-design.md:451-477 locks the glyph set and tames it with ONE CSS filter; the risk table (ui-design.md:514) guards only "emoji spam / inconsistent register" (quantity), and §7.4.1 R4 (prd.md ~4792) calls the register "locked low-risk (no asset pipeline)" mitigated by a single-platform capture-game-states sweep. No doc anywhere addresses cross-platform emoji glyph variance, tofu/monochrome fallback, or variation-selector (⛩️/🎐) failure — the technical claim is accurate (a uniform CSS filter over divergent per-OS base artwork cannot yield a consistent woodblock look, and the capture sweep runs on one platform so it structurally cannot detect this). This is a genuine, uncovered gap that collides with the first-class "look intentional, not AI-slop" pillar, and it is distinct from a curation/quantity concern. Downgraded from high to medium: emoji are aria-hidden and the kanji/word always carries the actual meaning (so no functional break — purely aesthetic), the remedy is already inside the permitted toolset (ui-design.md:4 permits inline SVG) and is a deferrable M6-era polish call, not a blocker. needs-human because resolving it amends the locked D-013 register and is a real design tradeoff, not clerical.
confirmedFindings.question: The locked TEXT+EMOJI+CSS art register makes system emoji load-bearing aesthetic carriers (pillar identity, season tags, rarity, log bullets), but a single Unicode codepoint renders as different artwork per OS/browser (Apple vs Google vs Windows vs Samsung), as monochrome/tofu where no colour-emoji font exists, and variation-selector glyphs (⛩️/🎐) can fail entirely — which the one shared CSS desaturation filter cannot reconcile, undermining the first-class coherent-woodblock / anti-slop pillar. Which path do we take, and which milestone owns it? Options: (A) bundle a controlled emoji font subset (Twemoji/Noto-Emoji) so every platform renders identical base glyphs — but this reintroduces an asset/font pipeline the R4 risk posture deliberately avoided; (B) replace the load-bearing period motifs (pillar/season/rarity marks) with the already-permitted inline-SVG path, leaving Unicode emoji only in roles where variance is cosmetically harmless — more authoring work but zero asset pipeline and full visual control; (C) accept platform variance as out-of-scope for v1 and revisit at M6. Whichever is chosen, should a cross-platform emoji-rendering check be added to the capture-game-states sweep (which today only samples one platform)?

confirmedFindings.title: Audio is a real asset pipeline with no licensing/credits, no autoplay-gesture handling, and contradicts the 'no-asset-pipeline' risk claim
confirmedFindings.evidence: §2.21 canon: "Audio (canon): light ambient beds + UI/event SFX + mute toggle"; M6 phase 7 requires "a light ambient audio bed + UI/event SFX (all behind mute)". Three unowned problems: (1) PROVENANCE/LICENSING — the PRD never says where the audio comes from, under what license, or that a credits/attribution surface exists (grep: zero hits for credits/attribution/royalty/CC-BY/composed/sourced). Shipping sourced audio on a public (even PWYW) itch page without attribution is a real legal exposure. (2) AUTOPLAY — browsers block audio playback until a user gesture, so an ambient bed cannot start on load; nothing specifies the start-on-first-interaction / muted-then-unmute pattern. (3) CONTRADICTION — §7.4.1 R4 justifies the art register as "low-risk… no asset pipeline: text + emoji + CSS", but audio files ARE an asset pipeline (sourcing, licensing, encoding, bundle size against the <5s load budget). None of this is reconciled.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: Legal/licensing and audio-autoplay are categories with no owning lens across three rounds; an unlicensed asset on a public release and a silently-broken ambient bed are both real launch defects.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Specify audio provenance and license (original / CC0 / OFL-equivalent), add a Credits/About attribution surface if any third-party assets are used, define the autoplay-unlock pattern (ambient bed starts muted or on first user gesture), and amend §7.4.1 R4 to acknowledge audio as a small but real asset pipeline (or scope audio out of v1 / make it optional).
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: The core sub-claim holds: the PRD specifies an audio feature (§2.21 line 1658 "light ambient beds + UI/event SFX + mute toggle"; M6 phase 7 line 4701) for a public PWYW itch.io release (canon §H), yet a grep confirms zero coverage of audio provenance, license, or a credits/attribution surface anywhere. The PRD is otherwise meticulous about provenance (it even adds a build-commit provenance stamp, line 4722) — so the omission of audio asset licensing is a genuine, un-owned gap with real (if easily-resolved) publishing exposure, and not a restatement of any rounds-1-2 visual/art finding. The other two bundled sub-claims are weak: the autoplay-gesture concern is self-solved (the cold open is a click via open_eyes and audio sits behind a mute toggle, so the bed naturally starts post-gesture — standard implementation, not a PRD decision), and the §7.4.1 R4 'no asset pipeline' line is scoped to the visual/art register, making the 'contradiction' pedantic. Net: real but low severity — audio is a small M6 polish item and the default fix (original or CC0/public-domain audio + a one-line credit) is near-free. Requires a human sourcing/licensing decision, so needs-human, not trivial-apply.
confirmedFindings.question: For v1 audio (light ambient beds + UI/event SFX, M6 phase 7), where do the assets come from and under what license — and does that require a Credits/About attribution surface? Options: (A) commission/generate ORIGINAL audio or use CC0/public-domain only → no attribution needed, no R4 change, lowest legal risk; (B) use third-party CC-BY/OFL-equivalent assets → must add a Credits line to the existing About surface (line 4722) and record the license; (C) scope audio OUT of v1 / make it a post-launch optional add → removes the concern entirely. Secondary: should §7.4.1 R4's 'no asset pipeline' wording be narrowed to 'no visual asset pipeline' to acknowledge audio as a small bundled asset class (bundle size counts against the <5s load budget)? (The autoplay-gesture pattern is an implementation detail handled by the click-driven cold open + mute toggle and does not need a PRD decision.)

confirmedFindings.title: `zip dist/` as written produces a broken itch.io upload (index.html must be at the archive root)
confirmedFindings.evidence: §7.3: "`npm run build:itch` = `vite build` + zip `dist/`. itch.io serves the unzipped bundle from a project subpath". itch.io's HTML-game host requires `index.html` at the ROOT of the uploaded zip; if the archive contains a top-level `dist/` folder (i.e. you zip the folder rather than its contents) the embed loads a blank frame and the game appears broken. The phrasing "zip `dist/`" (repeated at 3931, 4708, 4722, 4754) is ambiguous and, read literally, yields the nested-folder failure — one of the most common itch static-upload breaks, alongside the base-path issue the PRD does pin.
confirmedFindings.why_it_matters: A nested-folder zip is a silent, common deploy break that the M7 smoke test would surface late; pinning it next to the already-pinned relative-base fix removes a known static-host footgun.
confirmedFindings.suggested_resolution: Clarify the build:itch script to zip the CONTENTS of dist/ so index.html sits at the archive root.
confirmedFindings.verdict_reason: Verified at docs/prd.md:4754 (also 3931, 4708, 4722, 4751-52). The PRD repeatedly says "zip `dist/`". itch.io's HTML host requires index.html at the archive ROOT; `zip -r x.zip dist/` read literally nests everything under a top-level dist/ folder and the embed loads a blank frame — a genuine, common static-host break. The PRD already pins the relative-base break (rounds 1-2) but does NOT address the nested-folder break, so this is a distinct, new clarification, not a restatement. It is a factual correctness fix (zip contents vs folder), no design judgment → trivial-apply. Downgraded medium→low: M7 is a future milestone, this is doc-only, and the existing draft fresh-browser smoke-test step (4774) would surface it before publish.
confirmedFindings.old_string: `npm run build:itch` = `vite build` + zip `dist/`. itch.io serves the unzipped
confirmedFindings.new_string: `npm run build:itch` = `vite build` + zip **the *contents* of** `dist/` (so `index.html` sits at the **archive root**, never nested under a `dist/` folder — itch.io requires `index.html` at the zip root or the embed shows a blank frame). itch.io serves the unzipped

clusters.theme: T0 balance-proof integrity: the centrepiece gate proofs are internally invalid
clusters.summary: The two pillars of the T0 economic proof are broken. (1) The T0 Estate ≥0.8K gate proof spends 72 of its 560 deed-ip (12.9%) on 6 'sealed trade contracts', but the TRADE strand is a LOCKED T1 reveal ('no market in T0') — removing them drops the gate to 728 < 800, so the proof rounds 1-2 trusted is unreachable as itemized. (2) The per-rung koku throughput is labelled both NET (after food/re-investment → ~19K held) and GROSS (lifetime-produced → 3-5K held); the two readings are mutually exclusive, the held trajectory is modelled nowhere, and §4.7.5 proves E2@R6 affordability against lifetime-produced rather than held, so a R6 soft-lock cannot be ruled out.

clusters.theme: Implementation-blocking mechanic gaps: rules/magnitudes deferred to §4 but never delivered (M1/M2b blockers)
clusters.summary: Several core mechanics are declared in §2 and required by milestone DoD but have no codeable rule. (a) The Estate Service / Combat Standing rank-meters have no accrual rule and no per-rung crossing thresholds, yet M1 phase 4 must test rung flips — the T0 spine advance condition is unwritable. (b) The soft-stamina throttle function, per-action staminaCost, satiety drain, and rest recovery are all deferred to §4 and never defined, though §4.7.1's yield formula multiplies by '(soft-stamina rate)'. (c) §2.3/§6.3 say low satiety throttles combat, but §4.6.1's combat math has no satiety term (labour does) — affecting the LOCKED first-fight win-rate. (d) Equipment durability is stored and 'worn' but wear rate, at-zero behaviour, and stat scaling are undefined (M2b blocker). (e) The promised dent self-heal is mechanically impossible: the seasonal appraisal only fires on a strictly-new high-water, so a dented value (HW unchanged) can never refill via the appraisal as §4.2.4 claims.

clusters.theme: Persisted data model is narrower than the §2 type sketches; id-identity drift
clusters.summary: The canonical persisted shape (§6.4) cannot hold what the §2 systems declare. (a) influence stores one scalar per pillar, so the estateWealth land/treasury/trade sub-engines that the ≤⅓ clamp reads have no field. (b) CombatEncounterState — the one must-persist non-derivable mid-fight blob — is sketched nowhere, and the mid-fight verb payload is named two ways (CombatAction vs CombatInterventionIntent). (c) equip is dispatched with ItemId but stored as the undeclared EquipDefId with no mapping. (d) advance_dialogue references choiceId/ChoiceId but the dialogue-line type has no choices field. (e) toRung uses an undeclared RungId where the model uses RankId (clerical).

clusters.theme: World-sim weather/festival: declared, type-backed, RNG-backed, milestone-scheduled — but no magnitudes, no formula hook, and no persisted home
clusters.summary: Weather rate-modifiers and festival economic effects are promised v1 (T1) mechanics but §4 delivers zero magnitudes and the §4.7.1 yield / §4.6 combat formulas have no weather term, so M5 cannot implement them from the balance model. Separately, the current day's weather is a stochastic per-day draw that modifies live rates but has no field in GameState.clock — on active-only reload it is lost or re-rolled, breaking byte-identical resume. Both resolve together: is weather mechanical (needs magnitudes + persistence) or cosmetic-only in v1?

clusters.theme: Determinism guarantee has a Math.pow-sized hole the lint does not cover
clusters.summary: All growth curves use integer-exponent powers (XP_GROWTH^(L-1), r^owned, 1.25^tier), but ECMAScript guarantees correctly-rounded results only for +−×÷ and sqrt — Math.pow can differ by a ULP across engines/builds and a round() near x.5 then flips an integer level/cost threshold, desyncing the 'byte-identical cross-machine replay' promise. The §6.1 determinism lint bans nondeterministic inputs but not Math.pow/exp/log/trig. Practical break is limited to cross-engine save portability + an overclaimed promise (CI runs one engine).

The locked deploy target collides with locked design choices in a category no prior lens owned. (a) The 'robust' single IndexedDB autosave runs in itch's cross-origin iframe where third-party storage is partitioned (Chrome/Firefox) or blocked-by-default (Safari/WebKit), and the only backup (base64 export) is opt-in — a 28.5h save can be silently lost on Safari; M7's smoke test names no browser matrix. (b) Load-bearing system emoji render as different artwork per OS / as tofu where no colour-emoji font exists, defeating the 'consistent woodblock, not AI-slop' pillar that one CSS filter cannot rescue. (c) Audio is a real asset pipeline with no stated licensing/credits surface (legal exposure on a public release), contradicting the 'no asset pipeline' R4 claim. (d) 'zip dist/' read literally nests index.html under a folder → blank-frame itch upload (clerical clarify).

Documentation-completeness/drift surfaces. The §4.9 'complete tuning dashboard' omits the §4.3 conversion-weight coefficients and the §4.6.1 combat base constants (hpMax base/slope, skillBonus, accuracy/crit bases) that M6.2 must tune 'via §4.9 only' (both clerical cross-ref appends). The per-deed cap (0.04·band-top) and the four-pillar table are hand-restated in 4-6 places (latent drift traps); Name's per-deed-cap band-top (10K/80K) conflicts with its seasonal normalizer (15K/140K) and the cap formula cites a §4.1 gate Name lacks in v1; §6.6/§7 emit balance tables to docs/balance/ while CLAUDE.md says docs/content/; and the §4.8.0 'first reveal <30s' example names the R2 Skills tab instead of the koku panel (clerical).

clusters.theme: Narrative naming, kanji glyph, cast roster & loose thread hygiene
clusters.summary: Authorial naming/concordance nits, all low. Near-collisions co-occurring in the same scenes: antagonist Kuroiwa vs house Kurosawa (both Kuro-); legend-women Sayo/Obaa Sato; heir Naoyuki vs clerk Naozane (Nao-), plus Kageyuki/Naoyuki (-yuki) and Sōzaemon/Sōbei (Sō-). AGI is glyphed 体 ('body', a constitution semantic) where all its effects are dexterity (recommend 敏). §1.8 — the cited cast concordance anchor — omits recurring T4 spine figure Konoe (and others). Kanbei's v1-seeded 'player-influenceable détente/washout' has no resolution beat and no host system. Note several names sit in LOCKED canon, so renames reopen locked decisions.

Design-doc meta-voice and mechanics leak into in-fiction event-log copy, and a climax beat is duplicated. The V7 tier-capstone line says 'The room is clean' (the internal 'clean your room' design shorthand) as literal narration. Two T1 lines append non-diegetic parentheticals ('You may look, or not.'; 'Trade is one strand of one pillar — no more.'). The G6 spine line and the O5 Origin capstone share four near-verbatim sentences and fire at the same moment (O5 should DEEPEN, not echo), and the guaranteed G6 spine already speaks the 'Tahei' naming that §3.6.2 reserves for the optional Origin track — a payoff-ownership contradiction. The G0 dream-aside misattributes the ache-to-remember to Kanta's face rather than the MC.

clusters.theme: Genre-convention coverage vs the named inspirations
clusters.summary: Near-universal idle/incremental conventions both inspirations ship are absent: no bulk/sell-all/sell-to-floor or craft-N affordance (and no stated rule for how the saturation damper applies across a bulk sale), no player-facing achievements/career-stats surface (yet §6.4's counts comment says 'drive achievements' — a dangling reference; reword is clerical), and no in-game changelog/'what's new' panel (only an engineering commit-SHA stamp).

trivial_fixes.old_string: **< 30 s** (rice counter ticks → Skills tab fades in)
trivial_fixes.new_string: **< 30 s** (rice counter ticks → the koku row lights its own panel, §3.1)
trivial_fixes.rationale: §4.8.0's 'first reveal <30s' example names the Skills tab, which is an R2 reveal (tens of minutes in); the actual sub-30s reveal is the koku panel from the §3.1 cold open. The binding measurement column is unchanged.

trivial_fixes.new_string: **Conversion weights (§4.3):** the per-domain deed-base multipliers (`0.05·combatLevel`, `0.10·dangerRing`, `0.04·skillLevel`, `0.04·tradeSkill`, `0.06·officeRank`, `0.15·allianceSealed`, `0.25·Name-blend`). **Attributes:** all coefficients (§4.4), start = 5, +1 pt / 2 levels
trivial_fixes.rationale: The §4.9 'levers index' omits the §4.3 conversion-weight coefficients that §4.3 itself declares are levers; clerical cross-reference append of existing values.

RungId is referenced only here and never defined; the canonical id for a rung is RankId (§6.4/§6.5). Dead type drift that fails strict tsc; pure rename.
